proposals.title: Empirical Correlations for Dynamic Cone Penetrometer (DCP) Measurements with SPT, CPT, and Shear Wave Velocity in Gravelly Soils for Liquefaction Assessment
proposals.motivation: The Dynamic Cone Penetrometer (DCP) is a cost-effective and rapid tool for profiling soil strength, but its application for liquefaction assessment in gravelly soils remains underdeveloped. Standard methods rely heavily on SPT, CPT, and Vs data, which are more expensive and difficult to obtain in coarse-grained deposits. Developing robust correlations between DCP and these established in-situ tests would significantly enhance the feasibility of preliminary liquefaction screening and post-event reconnaissance in gravelly alluvial plains, such as those affected by the 2012 Emilia and 2020 earthquakes studied by Rollins.
proposals.hypothesis: That statistically significant, soil-type-dependent empirical correlations can be established between DCP penetration resistance (e.g., DCP blow count per unit penetration) and equivalent SPT N-values, CPT tip resistance (qt), and shear wave velocity (Vs) in gravelly soils, enabling the use of DCP data for preliminary liquefaction triggering analysis.
proposals.method: The research will compile a new database from well-documented gravelly soil sites, including co-located DCP, SPT, CPT, and seismic cone/downhole Vs measurements from post-earthquake investigations and research test sites. Statistical regression analysis (e.g., linear, power-law, and robust regression techniques) will be used to develop transformation models. The influence of gravel content, fines content, and soil gradation on the correlation coefficients will be explicitly analyzed. The derived correlations will be validated by applying them to independent case histories of liquefaction performance, comparing predicted liquefaction resistance curves with observed field behavior.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the geotechnical community with a validated, practical framework for interpreting DCP results in gravelly soils within the context of established liquefaction evaluation procedures. It will directly extend the use of low-cost DCP testing for preliminary hazard mapping and rapid post-earthquake assessment in coarse-grained deposits. The resulting database and correlations will fill a critical gap between routine site investigation and advanced in-situ testing for gravels.
proposals.novelty: While correlations exist for fine-grained soils, this study focuses explicitly on gravelly soils, where particle size effects significantly alter penetration mechanics, and systematically integrates DCP with all three primary in-situ test types (SPT, CPT, Vs) for liquefaction assessment, a synthesis not comprehensively attempted in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Regional-Scale Liquefaction Hazard Mapping of Gravelly Alluvial Plains Using a Dynamic Cone Penetrometer (DCP) Proxy Framework
proposals.motivation: Current regional liquefaction hazard maps, often based on SPT or CPT data, are poorly calibrated for gravelly soils due to equipment limitations and a scarcity of case histories. This creates significant uncertainty in seismic risk assessment for infrastructure built on Quaternary gravelly alluvial deposits, such as those in the Po Plain and similar regions globally. A practical, cost-effective method for characterizing these soils at a regional scale is urgently needed.
proposals.hypothesis: A regionally calibrated correlation between Dynamic Cone Penetrometer (DCP) blow counts and equivalent clean-sand corrected SPT (N1)60cs values can be established for gravelly soils, enabling the use of spatially extensive DCP test databases to generate reliable, first-order liquefaction hazard maps.
proposals.method: The research will compile a new database of collocated DCP and SPT/CPT soundings from post-earthquake investigations (e.g., 2012 Emilia, 2020 events) in gravelly alluvial settings. A site-specific correlation function (DCP-N value to (N1)60cs) will be developed using robust regression analysis, accounting for fines content and gradation. This correlation will be applied to existing regional geotechnical databases containing DCP data. Liquefaction potential index (LPI) calculations will be performed for a scenario earthquake using the DCP-derived (N1)60cs values, which will then be spatially interpolated using kriging to create probabilistic hazard maps.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, practical framework for generating liquefaction hazard maps in data-scarce gravelly soil regions. It will deliver a publicly accessible correlation model for gravels and a demonstration regional map (e.g., for a portion of the Po Plain), directly informing seismic microzonation efforts and land-use planning.
proposals.novelty: While DCP is used for local site assessment, its systematic upscaling to regional probabilistic hazard mapping for gravels is novel. The approach leverages existing, underutilized DCP datasets to fill a critical gap where traditional penetration test data is sparse.

proposals.title: Inverting the DCP-Based Liquefaction Triggering Procedure to Estimate Seismic Demand in Gravelly Soils
proposals.motivation: Current liquefaction assessment procedures are forward models, predicting liquefaction potential for a given earthquake loading. In post-earthquake forensic analysis, however, the in-situ state of the soil is known, but the precise ground motion at the site is often uncertain due to sparse instrumentation. An inverse procedure would allow engineers to back-calculate the seismic loading that caused observed liquefaction, improving the calibration of ground motion models and seismic hazard assessments for gravelly soils, which are prevalent in many seismically active alluvial plains.
proposals.hypothesis: The deterministic relationship between Dynamic Cone Penetrometer (DCP) blow counts, soil state, and cyclic resistance ratio (CRR) in established forward models can be inverted to solve for the cyclic stress ratio (CSR) required to trigger liquefaction, providing a reliable estimate of the causative seismic demand at a liquefied site.
proposals.method: The research will leverage existing DCP-based liquefaction triggering curves for gravelly soils, such as those derived from Rollins's prior case histories. The forward model (CRR = f(N_dcp, fines content, effective stress)) will be mathematically inverted. For sites with confirmed liquefaction, post-event DCP soundings and soil data will be used as input. The inverse algorithm will solve for the CSR at failure, which will then be deconvolved using established ground motion relations to estimate the peak ground acceleration (PGA) and moment magnitude (M_w) combination required to induce that CSR. The method will be validated against well-documented case histories from the 2012 Emilia and 2020 earthquake sequences where recorded motions are available.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel forensic tool for estimating ground motions at liquefied sites lacking instrumental records. It will contribute to a more robust database of 'observed' ground motions for gravelly soil sites, improving the constraints on seismic hazard analyses. The procedure will also enhance the interpretation of paleoliquefaction studies by providing a quantitative link between soil state and the intensity of past seismic events.
proposals.novelty: While DCP-based methods are established for forward liquefaction evaluation, their inversion to solve for seismic loading is a novel application. This approach moves beyond classification and forward prediction, repurposing the procedure as a diagnostic tool for seismic demand, a concept not explored in the pre-2026 literature for gravelly soils.

proposals.title: A Simplified CPT-Based Screening Method for Liquefaction Potential in Gravelly Soils Using a Normalized Soil Behavior Type Index
proposals.motivation: Current liquefaction assessment procedures, while detailed, are often too complex and data-intensive for rapid, preliminary hazard screening, especially for infrastructure planning and regional seismic risk studies. This is particularly problematic for gravelly soils, where standard penetration test (SPT)-based methods are less reliable and cone penetration test (CPT) data interpretation is more nuanced. A simplified, yet robust, screening tool is needed to efficiently identify zones requiring more detailed investigation.
proposals.hypothesis: A simplified, CPT-based screening method, calibrated against well-documented case histories of liquefaction and non-liquefaction in gravelly soils, can provide a reliable first-order identification of liquefaction hazard with minimal input parameters.
proposals.method: The method will develop a new, simplified liquefaction potential index (LPI_s) derived directly from CPT cone resistance (qc) and soil behavior type index (Ic). Using the comprehensive database from the 2012 Emilia earthquake and other pre-2026 case histories (e.g., 2020 events), we will establish threshold curves of normalized cone tip resistance (qc1N) versus Ic that separate liquefied from non-liquefied sites. The algorithm will involve normalizing qc for overburden pressure, calculating Ic, and comparing the data point to the developed threshold curves. The procedure will be validated against independent case histories not used in calibration.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide practicing engineers and geologists with a rapid, field-data-driven tool for preliminary liquefaction screening, specifically tailored to gravelly soils where SPT-based methods falter. It will translate complex case history databases into an accessible, graphical, or spreadsheet-based screening method, improving the efficiency of geotechnical seismic hazard assessments.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in distilling the complex, multi-parameter CPT-based liquefaction evaluation frameworks into a single, normalized chart specifically for gravelly soils, bypassing the need for fines content correction and stress reduction coefficient calculations in the initial screening phase.

proposals.title: Re-evaluating Triggering Curves for Gravelly Soils: A Data-Driven Analysis of the 2012 Emilia Earthquake Liquefaction Database
proposals.motivation: Current simplified procedures for assessing liquefaction triggering, largely based on case histories in sandy soils, are often applied to gravelly soils despite significant uncertainties. The 2020 and 2012 Italian earthquake sequences provided extensive field performance data for gravelly alluvial deposits, yet a systematic, data-centric analysis to calibrate or challenge existing triggering models for these soils is lacking. This gap hinders reliable seismic risk assessment in regions underlain by coarse-grained alluvium.
proposals.hypothesis: The compiled geological and geotechnical database from the 2012 Emilia earthquake will reveal that existing stress-based liquefaction triggering curves (e.g., Robertson and Wride, 1998; Boulanger and Idriss, 2014) are non-conservative for gravelly soils when using equivalent clean-sand corrected cone penetration test (CPT) parameters, necessitating a new, data-constrained model.
proposals.method: The research will conduct a detailed statistical re-analysis of the published Emilia earthquake database, focusing on sites with gravelly soils. The core method involves calculating equivalent clean-sand cone tip resistance (qc1N)cs and cyclic stress ratio (CSR) for each case history. We will apply Bayesian updating techniques to existing probabilistic triggering models (e.g., Cetin et al., 2004 framework) using the Emilia dataset as the likelihood function. This will quantify the shift in the median triggering curve and its uncertainty. The analysis will stratify data by geological unit (e.g., paleo-channel vs. levee deposits) and fines content to identify controlling factors.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a revised, empirically grounded probabilistic triggering curve specifically calibrated for gravelly soils of the Po Plain and analogous geologic settings. It will deliver quantitative guidance on the adjustment factors needed for applying standard CPT-based methods to gravels. The findings will directly improve the accuracy of liquefaction hazard maps and engineering evaluations in regions with coarse-grained deposits.
proposals.novelty: While databases exist, this proposal uniquely applies a formal Bayesian inference framework to rigorously update state-of-practice liquefaction models using a high-quality, region-specific dataset for gravels. It moves beyond qualitative comparison to provide a statistically quantifiable revision of triggering relationships, explicitly linking geological context to model parameters.

proposals.title: Centrifuge Modeling of Seismic Response and Liquefaction in Gravelly Alluvial Sequences of the Po Plain: Validation Against the 2012 Emilia Earthquake Case Histories
proposals.motivation: The 2012 Emilia earthquakes provided a rich database of liquefaction case histories in the gravelly soils of the Po Plain, yet current empirical liquefaction triggering procedures (e.g., CPT-based methods) are primarily calibrated for sandy soils. The performance of these methods for gravelly soils remains uncertain, and high-quality physical models of these complex stratigraphies under seismic loading are lacking. A validated physical model is essential to improve predictive tools for critical infrastructure in similar alluvial environments.
proposals.hypothesis: That the stratigraphic complexity of the Po Plain's Quaternary alluvial deposits—specifically the interbedded gravelly, sandy, and silty layers—fundamentally controls the seismic response and liquefaction manifestation, and that this system response can be accurately replicated and studied through scaled centrifuge testing.
proposals.method: We will construct a detailed 1-g physical model of a representative Emilia stratigraphic column, using soil types (clean gravels, sandy gravels, silty sands) characterized from the 2012 earthquake database. This model will be instrumented with pore pressure transducers and accelerometers and tested in a geotechnical centrifuge at 50-80g. In-flight seismic loading will be applied using a hydraulic shaker, replicating scaled versions of the 2012 Emilia motions. The primary analysis will involve comparing the model's excess pore pressure generation, acceleration response, and post-shaking settlements directly with the field case history data and in-situ test (CPT) curves from liquefied and non-liquefied sites.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a benchmark centrifuge dataset for liquefaction in gravelly, stratified alluvial systems, directly tied to a well-documented field event. It will provide a physical basis for evaluating and refining CPT-based liquefaction triggering correlations for gravelly soils. The results will advance the mechanistic understanding of how interbedded layering influences pore pressure dissipation and surface manifestation.
proposals.novelty: While centrifuge testing of liquefaction is common, this proposal uniquely focuses on replicating the specific, documented stratigraphy of a major gravelly soil liquefaction event (Emilia 2012) to perform a direct, quantitative validation of model response against comprehensive field case histories and in-situ test data.

proposals.title: Inverting Liquefaction Manifestation Maps to Constrain Spatially-Variable Ground Motions: A Case Study of the 2012 Emilia Earthquake Sequence
proposals.motivation: Post-earthquake liquefaction maps are a direct, widespread record of strong ground shaking, yet they are rarely used quantitatively to constrain shaking intensity where instrumental records are sparse. This inverse problem is critical for improving seismic hazard assessment in regions like the Po Plain, Italy, which have dense infrastructure and complex, gravelly alluvial stratigraphy. A robust method to back-calculate shaking from liquefaction would provide valuable ground-truth for validating and refining ground motion models.
proposals.hypothesis: The spatial distribution and severity of liquefaction manifestations (e.g., sand boils, lateral spreads) can be inverted using a Bayesian framework to produce probabilistic estimates of peak ground acceleration (PGA) and shaking duration across an affected region, with uncertainty bounds that reflect geotechnical variability.
proposals.method: We will compile a high-resolution geospatial database of liquefaction features from the 2012 Emilia earthquakes, integrating the existing comprehensive database with new field data. For each site, we will use in-situ test data (CPT, SPT) to perform forward liquefaction triggering analyses using stress-based methods (e.g., Boulanger & Idriss, 2014) for a range of input PGAs. We will then implement a Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) algorithm to solve the inverse problem: finding the set of shaking intensities across the grid that, when used in the forward model, best matches the observed pattern of liquefaction severity. The method will explicitly incorporate the uncertainty in soil properties, particularly for gravelly soils, using probabilistic SPT-CPT correlations.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, geotechnically-grounded methodology for extracting quantitative shaking estimates from geological evidence. It will produce a spatially-variable shaking map for the Emilia sequence, independent of instrumental records, for comparison with seismological models. The framework will advance paleoseismology by providing a more rigorous tool for interpreting prehistoric liquefaction features.
proposals.novelty: While forward liquefaction prediction is standard, the systematic Bayesian inversion of entire liquefaction maps to constrain shaking is a significant paradigm shift. The approach moves beyond single-point empirical back-calculations by integrating spatial correlation of soil properties and shaking, offering a more comprehensive solution to the inverse problem.

proposals.title: A Critical Assessment of Blast-Induced Liquefaction as a Proxy for Seismic Loading in Gravelly Soils: A Field and Laboratory Investigation
proposals.motivation: Blast-induced liquefaction is a common field testing technique to assess soil liquefaction potential, predicated on the assumption that it replicates seismic cyclic loading. However, the fundamental differences in strain paths, loading rates, and pore pressure generation/dissipation mechanisms between blast (shock) and earthquake (cyclic) loading remain poorly quantified, especially for gravelly soils. This gap is critical because gravelly soils, often treated as non-liquefiable, have shown liquefaction in recent earthquakes (e.g., 2012 Emilia), and reliance on an imperfect simulation could lead to unsafe or overly conservative engineering assessments.
proposals.hypothesis: The pore pressure generation, dissipation, and resultant stress-strain response of saturated gravelly soils under blast loading are fundamentally different from those under true seismic cyclic loading, leading to non-conservative or misleading correlations when blast tests are used to calibrate seismic liquefaction triggering curves.
proposals.method: The research will employ a coupled field and laboratory program. First, controlled blast tests will be performed at a well-characterized gravelly site, with extensive in-situ monitoring using piezometers, CPTu, and SCPTu before and after blasting to capture pore pressure generation and dissipation. Concurrently, high-quality frozen samples from the same site will be subjected to stress-controlled cyclic direct simple shear (DSS) and cyclic triaxial tests in the lab to establish a benchmark seismic response. The key analysis will involve comparing the time-frequency characteristics of the loadings, the normalized pore pressure ratio (ru) build-up curves, and the post-triggering strain accumulation, using statistical methods (e.g., Welch's t-test) to quantify significant differences in the response parameters.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, high-quality dataset comparing blast and cyclic loading responses for gravelly soils, leading to improved guidelines on the applicability and limitations of blast testing. It will advance the physical understanding of liquefaction mechanisms in gravels under different loading types and contribute to more reliable, performance-based seismic hazard assessment for critical infrastructure founded on such soils.
proposals.novelty: While blast testing is widely used, its validity as a direct analog for seismic loading is often assumed rather than rigorously tested. This study uniquely challenges this paradigm by focusing on gravelly soils—a complex material where the assumption is most tenuous—and employs a direct comparative framework using parallel field and advanced laboratory data, moving beyond empirical correlation.

proposals.title: Inversion of Post-Liquefaction Settlement to Constrain Pre-Event State Parameters in Gravelly Soils
proposals.motivation: Current liquefaction assessment relies heavily on pre-event in-situ tests (e.g., CPT, SPT), which are often unavailable for sites that have already liquefied. This creates a critical gap in forensic analysis and the calibration of empirical models, particularly for gravelly soils where case histories are sparse. A method to back-calculate pre-liquefaction soil state from the readily observable consequence—ground settlement—would significantly enhance post-event investigations and database development.
proposals.hypothesis: The post-liquefaction volumetric strain and surface settlement pattern contain a unique signature that can be inverted, using a calibrated constitutive model, to estimate key pre-event state parameters such as relative density, initial shear stress, and confining pressure.
proposals.method: The method will leverage the extensive case history database from the 2012 Emilia and 2020 earthquake sequences, where detailed post-event settlements and pre-event cone penetration test (CPT) data are available. A Bayesian inverse analysis framework will be developed, coupling a critical state-based constitutive model (e.g., PM4Sand) with a Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) sampler. The forward model will simulate cyclic stress response and resulting volumetric strain; the MCMC algorithm will iteratively adjust the prior distributions of input state parameters to minimize the misfit between simulated and observed settlement profiles.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, practical tool for forensic geotechnical engineering, allowing researchers to extract valuable soil state data from past liquefaction events where direct measurements are absent. It will directly improve the calibration of liquefaction triggering and consequence models for gravelly soils by expanding the effective database of well-constrained case histories.
proposals.novelty: While forward modeling of settlement is common, the explicit inverse analysis to deduce pre-liquefaction state from settlement is a non-trivial, underexplored problem. The application of a Bayesian inversion framework to this geotechnical problem, specifically targeting gravelly soils with their complex behavior, moves beyond traditional deterministic back-analysis.

proposals.title: A Bayesian Updating Framework for Probabilistic Urban Liquefaction Hazard Maps Using CPT and SPT Case Histories
proposals.motivation: Current liquefaction hazard maps often rely on simplified geological units or sparse data, lacking the spatial resolution needed for urban risk management. The 2012 Emilia and 2020 earthquake sequences in Italy highlighted the catastrophic potential of liquefaction in alluvial plains, underscoring the need for high-resolution, data-driven hazard assessment. Scaling point-specific in-situ test data (e.g., CPT, SPT) to a city-wide probabilistic framework remains a critical, unsolved challenge in geotechnical earthquake engineering.
proposals.hypothesis: That a geostatistical Bayesian updating framework, which integrates spatially correlated cone penetration test (CPT) data with regional case history databases (including gravelly soils), can generate probabilistic liquefaction hazard maps with quantified uncertainty that are significantly more accurate than maps based on geology alone.
proposals.method: The method will compile a dense geospatial database of CPT/SPT soundings and liquefaction performance case histories from studied regions (e.g., Po Plain). A primary variogram model will characterize the spatial correlation of key parameters like tip resistance (qt). A Bayesian updating algorithm (e.g., Sequential Gaussian Simulation coupled with Bayesian kriging) will be employed. This algorithm will treat existing liquefaction triggering curves (e.g., Idriss & Boulanger, 2014) as prior models and update the probability of liquefaction across a grid by conditioning on both the measured point data and the spatial correlation structure.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a reproducible, open-source methodology for generating physics-informed, probabilistic liquefaction hazard maps at the urban scale. It will directly contribute a validated framework for integrating sparse field test data into regional assessments, explicitly including the challenging case of gravelly soils. The output will be a significant advancement towards performance-based earthquake engineering for infrastructure systems.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit fusion of geostatistical simulation with Bayesian updating of liquefaction triggering models using high-density in-situ test data, moving beyond deterministic factor-of-safety contours to full-field probabilistic hazard maps with uncertainty bounds. This approach is specifically tailored to incorporate the complex spatial variability captured in modern CPT campaigns.

proposals.title: Inverting the Liquefaction Resistance Framework: A Mechanistic Model to Predict Dynamic Penetration Test (DPT) Blow Counts from Soil State and Cyclic Resistance
proposals.motivation: Current practice for assessing liquefaction in gravelly soils relies heavily on empirical correlations between in-situ test indices (like DPT blow counts) and cyclic resistance. These correlations are inherently site-specific and lack a direct physical basis, leading to uncertainty, especially in non-plastic, coarse-grained soils. A model that inverts this relationship, predicting the in-situ test response from fundamental soil state and resistance parameters, would provide a more mechanistic and generalizable framework for liquefaction hazard evaluation.
proposals.hypothesis: The blow count from a Dynamic Penetration Test (DPT) in cohesionless soils can be robustly predicted by a constitutive model that integrates the in-situ state (e.g., relative density, confining pressure) and the soil's intrinsic cyclic resistance ratio (CRR), thereby inverting the standard empirical liquefaction evaluation procedure.
proposals.method: The research will leverage Dr. Rollins's existing databases of field case histories, including DPT soundings and post-liquefaction observations from events like the 2012 Emilia and 2020 earthquakes. A numerical model of the DPT penetration process will be developed using a critical-state compatible constitutive model (e.g., a modified SANISAND-type framework) implemented in a 1D finite difference code. The model will be calibrated using laboratory-derived cyclic resistance parameters and in-situ state data from selected case histories. A Bayesian updating algorithm will be employed to inversely determine the model parameters that best predict the recorded DPT N-values from the known CRR and stress state.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a first-of-its-kind, physics-informed model that quantitatively links cyclic resistance to penetration resistance, moving beyond purely statistical correlations. It will provide a tool for interpreting DPT results in a more fundamental manner and for reconciling discrepancies between different in-situ test methods in gravelly soils. The model framework could eventually be extended to improve liquefaction triggering assessments in database-poor regions.
proposals.novelty: While extensive work exists to predict CRR from blow counts, the explicit inversion—using CRR and state parameters to predict the penetration test response—is a fundamentally novel and untested approach. It shifts the paradigm from a purely empirical correlation to a simulated soil-mechanics experiment anchored in constitutive behavior.

proposals.title: Laboratory Calibration of Dynamic Penetration Test (DPT) Blow Counts for Liquefaction Assessment of Gravelly Soils
proposals.motivation: Current liquefaction assessment procedures for gravelly soils rely heavily on empirical correlations from case histories, primarily using the Standard Penetration Test (SPT) or Cone Penetration Test (CPT). The Dynamic Penetration Test (DPT) is a cost-effective alternative for gravels, but its blow count (N_d) lacks a direct, physically calibrated relationship with cyclic resistance. This gap limits the reliable use of DPT in engineering practice, particularly in regions like the Po Plain where gravelly alluvium is common.
proposals.hypothesis: A robust, laboratory-derived correlation can be established between DPT blow counts (N_d) and the cyclic stress ratio (CSR) required to trigger liquefaction in reconstituted gravelly soils, accounting for key factors like fines content and relative density.
proposals.method: We will perform a series of large-scale cyclic simple shear tests on reconstituted specimens of clean and silty gravels. Specimens will be prepared at controlled relative densities in a large-diameter mold (e.g., 300 mm). Prior to cyclic shearing, each specimen will be characterized using a miniature, instrumented DPT probe driven within the chamber to measure N_d. The subsequent cyclic loading will establish the CSR causing 3% double-amplitude shear strain. The experimental matrix will systematically vary relative density (40-80%), fines content (0-20%), and confining stress (50-150 kPa). The resulting dataset of paired (N_d, CSR) will be analyzed using regression techniques to develop a probabilistic boundary curve, analogous to the SPT-based curves by Idriss and Boulanger (2008).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct physical calibration of DPT for gravels, moving its application beyond purely empirical case-history matching. It will yield a new, laboratory-verified correlation curve for engineers to assess liquefaction resistance in gravels using DPT data. The results will enhance the reliability of seismic hazard evaluations in gravel-dominated alluvial settings.
proposals.novelty: While field case histories exist, no study has directly and systematically measured the cyclic resistance of a gravel specimen immediately after an in-situ DPT simulation in the lab. This controlled approach isolates the fundamental soil property relationship, free from the uncertainties of field heterogeneity and groundwater conditions inherent in case-history databases.

proposals.title: Inverse Estimation of Site-Specific Peak Ground Acceleration from Dynamic Penetration Test Data and Observed Liquefaction Performance
proposals.motivation: Current practice for assessing seismic hazard and liquefaction triggering relies on estimated or regionally modeled peak ground acceleration (PGA). This introduces significant uncertainty into post-earthquake forensic analyses and the calibration of empirical methods, particularly for gravelly soils where case histories are sparse. A robust, data-driven method to back-calculate the actual shaking intensity at a site from field performance and in-situ test data would greatly improve the reliability of liquefaction case history databases and subsequent model development.
proposals.hypothesis: For a well-documented site with known liquefaction performance (yes/no) and high-quality Dynamic Penetration Test (DPT) profiles, the site-specific PGA that triggered the observed field response can be reliably estimated by inverting state-of-practice liquefaction triggering procedures.
proposals.method: The research will leverage the researcher's existing databases of liquefaction case histories, particularly from the 2012 Emilia and 2020 events, focusing on sites with DPT (e.g., SPT, CPTu) data. The inverse framework will be built around the Cyclic Stress Ratio (CSR) formulation from Idriss and Boulanger (2014) or a similar pre-2026 model. For each case, we will solve for the PGA that, when used to calculate CSR, yields a factor of safety (FS) closest to 1.0 at the critical layer, consistent with the observed performance. A Bayesian updating algorithm will be employed to incorporate uncertainties in magnitude scaling factors, stress reduction coefficients (rd), and soil behavior type indices, producing probabilistic estimates of the causative PGA for each case history.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a new dataset of back-calculated, site-specific PGAs for well-instrumented case histories, reducing the epistemic uncertainty in ground motion inputs for these cases. It will provide a formalized methodology for forensic seismic intensity estimation, directly benefiting the calibration of next-generation liquefaction triggering models. The results will be particularly valuable for constraining shaking in gravelly soils, where the relationship between penetration resistance and cyclic resistance remains an active research area.
proposals.novelty: While liquefaction evaluation is a classic forward problem, systematically inverting the procedure to solve for the input seismic demand from field performance is a novel application. This approach moves beyond using estimated PGAs from ground motion models, instead treating them as unknown parameters to be solved using the soil itself as a 'recorder' of the shaking intensity.

proposals.title: The Engineered Heterogeneous Crust: Assessing the Composite Barrier Effect of Ground Improvement in the Absence of a Natural Non-Liquefiable Layer
proposals.motivation: Current seismic design codes for liquefaction mitigation often assume the presence of a competent, non-liquefiable crust to prevent damaging surface effects. Many sites, particularly in alluvial and reclaimed areas, lack this natural geologic feature, forcing overly conservative or costly foundation solutions. This research addresses a critical gap by evaluating whether engineered ground improvement itself can fulfill the crust's role, expanding viable mitigation strategies for challenging sites.
proposals.hypothesis: The spatially heterogeneous composite formed by discrete ground improvement elements (e.g., stone columns, jet-grouted columns) creates an effective hydraulic and mechanical barrier that impedes pore pressure dissipation and redistracts shear strains, thereby preventing surface manifestation of liquefaction even in the absence of a natural, continuous non-liquefiable crust.
proposals.method: The research will combine detailed case history analysis from post-earthquake investigations (e.g., 2012 Emilia, 2020 events) with advanced numerical modeling. Finite element models (using software like OpenSees or PLAXIS) will explicitly represent the 3D heterogeneity of improved soil grids. The models will be calibrated against in-situ test data (CPT, SPT) and piezometer records from liquefied sites. Parametric studies will systematically vary improvement ratio, column stiffness contrast, and layout randomness to quantify their influence on pore pressure redistribution and surface settlement using coupled hydromechanical analysis.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new framework for analyzing composite ground improvement systems as engineered crustal substitutes, moving beyond uniform-method equivalency. It will yield practical design charts linking improvement parameters to barrier effectiveness. The findings could directly inform revisions to code provisions (e.g., NCEER, Eurocode) for seismic design on improved ground lacking a natural crust.
proposals.novelty: It fundamentally shifts perspective by treating the inherent spatial heterogeneity and discontinuity of ground improvement not as a flaw but as a potential system-level design feature that can mimic a specific, favorable geologic condition.

proposals.title: 3D Tomographic Imaging of Ground Improvement in Gravelly Soils: A Geology-Specific Framework Integrating Surface-Wave Methods and Dynamic Cone Penetrometer Validation
proposals.motivation: Assessing ground improvement in gravelly soils for liquefaction mitigation remains a significant challenge. Current methods, like SPT or CPT, are often limited in gravels and provide only 1D point measurements, failing to capture the volumetric heterogeneity of improvement. A robust, 3D characterization method is needed to verify improvement effectiveness across diverse geologic settings like alluvial, glacial, and colluvial deposits, which have distinct fabrics influencing treatment response.
proposals.hypothesis: The 3D spatial pattern of shear wave velocity (Vs) improvement in treated gravelly soils, derived from surface-wave tomography, is systematically linked to the intrinsic soil fabric and depositional environment. This relationship can be quantified to create geology-specific predictive models for improvement verification.
proposals.method: The research will employ active and passive multichannel analysis of surface waves (MASW) to perform 2D/3D surface-wave tomography across improvement zones in at least three distinct geologic settings. The resulting 3D Vs volumes will be calibrated and validated against a dense spatial array of Dynamic Cone Penetrometer (DCP) tests, using the DCP penetration index (DPI) as a direct measure of penetration resistance. A geostatistical framework (e.g., kriging with external drift) will be used to fuse the tomographic and DCP data, creating high-resolution 3D models of improved soil properties.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, practical framework for the volumetric assessment of ground improvement in gravels, moving beyond point-based verification. It will produce the first set of geology-specific correlations between 3D geophysical images and in-situ penetration resistance for improved gravelly soils, directly extending the DCP-based assessment framework established in prior work.
proposals.novelty: The fusion of 3D surface-wave tomography with a spatially distributed DCP network for ground improvement assessment is novel. Furthermore, explicitly linking the 3D spatial patterns of improvement to depositional geology to create predictive models represents a significant advance beyond empirical, site-specific correlations.

proposals.title: A Multi-Branch Neural Network with Attention for Liquefaction Vulnerability Assessment by Fusing CPTu, DMT, and Shear Wave Velocity Data
proposals.motivation: Current liquefaction assessment methods, including probabilistic models, often rely on a single in-situ test type (e.g., CPT), potentially missing complementary information from other tests. This can lead to uncertainty, particularly in complex or gravelly soils where penetration-based methods have limitations. A robust framework to intelligently integrate heterogeneous geotechnical data is needed to improve prediction reliability for critical infrastructure.
proposals.hypothesis: A specialized neural network architecture that fuses CPTu, DMT, and Vs data through an attention mechanism will outperform single-method and traditional ensemble models in predicting liquefaction triggering, as it can dynamically weight the most informative signals from each test for a given soil profile.
proposals.method: We will develop a multi-branch neural network. Each branch—comprising convolutional or dense layers—will pre-process raw or derived features from one test type (CPTu, DMT, Vs). The encoded features from all branches will be fed into a central fusion layer employing a multi-head attention mechanism (inspired by Transformer architectures) to learn context-dependent weighting. The model will be trained and validated using a compiled global database of case histories, including gravelly soil sites from events like the 2012 Emilia earthquake.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, data-driven framework for multi-test liquefaction assessment that quantifies the relative contribution of different in-situ methods. It will deliver a more accurate and explainable tool for practitioners and advance the understanding of how complementary geotechnical data synergistically informs soil behavior under seismic loading.
proposals.novelty: This is the first application of an attention-based multi-modal fusion network, a concept advanced in medical imaging, to geotechnical data integration. It moves beyond simple averaging or heuristic weighting to a learned, context-aware fusion of CPTu, DMT, and Vs data.

proposals.title: A Joint Inversion Framework for 4D Liquefaction Characterization in Gravelly Soils: Integrating InSAR Surface Deformation with Seismic Tomography
proposals.motivation: Current liquefaction assessment relies on spatially sparse in-situ tests (e.g., CPT, SPT) and geophysical surveys, creating gaps in understanding the 4D evolution of pore pressure and void ratio redistribution. This limits accurate validation of remediation techniques and post-event forensic analysis. A method to generate continuous, time-lapse models of subsurface state changes is critically needed for improved hazard assessment.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating time-series surface deformation data (a direct manifestation of volumetric strain) as a constraint in the geophysical inversion process will yield a more accurate, high-resolution 4D model of evolving subsurface properties during and after liquefaction than either dataset can provide independently.
proposals.method: The framework will jointly invert Sentinel-1 InSAR-derived surface displacement time-series and active-source seismic tomography (P- and S-wave) data collected pre- and post-liquefaction. A coupled hydro-mechanical model will link subsurface property changes (e.g., Vs, porosity) to surface deformation. The joint inversion will be solved using a Bayesian approach or a structural constraint technique, minimizing a cost function that incorporates data misfit for both geophysical and deformation datasets, regularized by prior information from case history databases.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, validated workflow for 4D subsurface imaging of liquefaction effects, directly translating to improved performance assessment of ground improvement techniques like densification. It will advance the state-of-practice by providing continuous, quantitative maps of critical state parameters beyond discrete sounding locations, enhancing forensic analysis and risk models.
proposals.novelty: While joint inversion is used in geophysics, its application to constrain subsurface models with *surface deformation* specifically for engineering liquefaction characterization is novel. This moves beyond correlative comparisons to a physically integrated solution for a persistent problem in geotechnical earthquake engineering.

proposals.title: Calibrating In-Situ Test Interpretation for Gravelly Soils via Fabric-Controlled Synthetic Specimens and Natural Case History Validation
proposals.motivation: Current empirical correlations for interpreting in-situ tests (e.g., CPTu, SDMT) in gravelly soils for liquefaction assessment are largely based on clean sands, neglecting the significant influence of fabric, stress history, and minor fines. This leads to high uncertainty in seismic risk evaluations, as evidenced by case histories from the 2012 Emilia and 2020 earthquakes in alluvial deposits. A fundamental, mechanics-based calibration framework is urgently needed.
proposals.hypothesis: The calibration of in-situ test parameters (e.g., normalized tip resistance, shear wave velocity) against fundamental state properties can be significantly improved by using synthetic soil specimens engineered to replicate the fabric, aging, and partial saturation effects of natural gravelly alluvial deposits.
proposals.method: The research employs a dual-path approach. First, synthetic specimens of gravelly sand with silty fines will be created using moist tamping and pluviation under controlled stresses, with optional cementing agents, to engineer specific fabric and stress history. Second, a comprehensive database of high-quality paired in-situ test data (CPTu/SDMT) and laboratory index/state properties from well-documented natural sites (e.g., the 2012 Emilia liquefaction database) will be compiled. Advanced statistical and machine learning techniques (e.g., Bayesian calibration, random forest regression) will be used to develop and validate new correlations by comparing the response of synthetic specimens to the natural benchmark.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, validated calibration framework that explicitly accounts for fabric and history, moving beyond purely empirical approaches. It will provide revised correlation curves for interpreting CPTu and SDMT data in gravelly soils of the Po Plain and similar alluvial settings, directly improving the reliability of liquefaction triggering assessments.
proposals.novelty: The explicit creation of synthetic soil models as 'calibration targets' for in-situ test interpretation is a paradigm shift from purely empirical or numerical calibration. It directly bridges the gap between controlled laboratory fabrication and the complex, history-dependent features captured in field case histories.

proposals.title: A Probabilistic Performance-Based Inverse Framework for the Design of Ground Improvement in Liquefiable Gravelly Soils
proposals.motivation: Current ground improvement design for liquefaction mitigation, particularly in gravelly soils, is often prescriptive and not explicitly linked to quantitative performance outcomes like post-earthquake settlement. This leads to potentially inefficient or over-conservative designs. A methodology that directly calculates improvement specifications needed to meet a site-specific deformation limit would represent a significant advance in performance-based geotechnical earthquake engineering.
proposals.hypothesis: A site-specific probabilistic model linking in-situ test parameters (e.g., CPT qc, Vs) to post-liquefaction deformation can be inverted to uniquely define the ground improvement scheme required to achieve a target performance level, moving design from a trial-and-error to a direct calculation process.
proposals.method: The research will first compile a curated database from case histories (e.g., 2012 Emilia, 2020 events) to develop Bayesian regression models predicting settlement/lateral spread as a function of SPT, CPT, and Vs measurements for untreated ground. For the inverse solver, a metaheuristic algorithm (e.g., Particle Swarm Optimization) will be employed. It will iteratively adjust design variables (e.g., stone column grid spacing, depth, modulus) within a coupled numerical model (e.g., FLAC with PM4Silt/PM4Sand) until the simulated post-improvement soil state, when fed into the probabilistic deformation model, predicts deformations below the target threshold.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, closed-loop design framework that quantitatively links ground improvement geometry and properties to a probabilistic performance target. It will provide practicing engineers with a more rational and transparent design tool for liquefaction mitigation, specifically addressing the complex behavior of gravelly soils. The resulting methodology will be validated against documented case histories of improved sites.
proposals.novelty: The fusion of a field-data-derived probabilistic deformation model with an inverse optimization engine to *directly solve* for ground improvement specifications is a fundamental shift from current iterative forward-design approaches. This explicitly connects the treatment design to a quantified risk of deformation, which is the ultimate concern for infrastructure.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Intrusive Testing Disturbance in Improved Gravelly Soils: A Coupled Seismic Cone Penetrometer and Surface Wave Tomography Protocol
proposals.motivation: Current practice for verifying ground improvement, especially for liquefaction mitigation in gravelly soils, relies heavily on intrusive in-situ tests like the Seismic Dilatometer (SDMT) and Cone Penetration Test (CPTu). The act of penetrating the improved ground with these tools may itself induce localized disturbance, remolding, or pore pressure changes, potentially biasing the very measurements intended to assess improvement quality. This 'observer effect' introduces uncertainty into performance verification, a critical gap given the extensive use of these methods in post-earthquake reconnaissance and design validation.
proposals.hypothesis: The penetration of an SDMT or CPTu probe into recently improved, partially saturated gravelly soils creates a measurable, localized zone of disturbance that alters small-strain stiffness and density parameters. This disturbance can be quantified by comparing pre- and post-penetration shear wave velocity (Vs) profiles obtained from a truly non-intrusive surface method.
proposals.method: The protocol employs a sequential, same-footprint testing approach at sites with gravelly soils treated via vibro-compaction or dynamic compaction. First, a high-resolution 2D/3D surface wave tomography (MASW/ReMi) survey establishes a baseline Vs profile. Immediately following, a Seismic Cone Penetration Test (SCPTu) is performed at the survey center, providing concurrent tip resistance, sleeve friction, pore pressure, and downhole Vs measurements. The surface wave tomography is then repeated. The difference between the pre- and post-intrusion surface-derived Vs models will delineate the disturbance zone. A comparative analysis will correlate this disturbance with SCPTu data and soil index properties using multivariate regression models.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a first-order quantification of intrusive testing disturbance in improved gravelly ground, leading to more reliable interpretation of verification test data. It will yield a practical framework for decoupling test-induced effects from the true ground state, enhancing the reliability of post-improvement assessment. The resulting dataset will also improve calibration of numerical models simulating cone penetration in heterogeneous, coarse-grained soils.
proposals.novelty: While disturbance is acknowledged in soft clays, its systematic study in improved gravels—where verification is paramount—is lacking. The novel coupling of surface wave tomography as a before-and-after diagnostic tool to audit the intrusive testing process itself provides a direct, geophysical analogy to non-perturbative measurement techniques from other fields.

proposals.title: A Closed-Loop Framework for Performance-Based Verification of Engineered Granular Fills Using Calibrated In-Situ Test Disturbance
proposals.motivation: Engineered gravelly fills are critical for seismic isolation and vibration-sensitive foundations, but verifying their as-built dynamic properties (e.g., shear modulus, G_max, and damping) remains a challenge. Conventional intrusive tests like SPT or CPTu disturb the very fabric they aim to measure, while non-intrusive methods like SASW lack the resolution for thin layers. A method to quantify and utilize this disturbance for quality assurance is needed to ensure fills meet design performance targets.
proposals.hypothesis: The disturbance signature imparted by a penetration test (e.g., SDMT or seismic CPTu) in a compacted granular fill is a systematic function of the fill's initial state (gradation, density, stress). This signature can be characterized and inverted to predict the pre-disturbance, in-situ dynamic properties and guide corrective compaction.
proposals.method: The research will conduct a controlled field program on instrumented test fills of varying gradations (well-graded to gap-graded) and densities. Pre- and post-penetration dynamic properties will be measured using paired in-situ tests: Seismic Dilatometer (SDMT) tests for G_max and damping, complemented by cross-hole seismic surveys. A machine learning model (e.g., a gradient-boosted regression tree) will be trained to correlate the test-induced change in dynamic properties (ΔG_max, ΔD) with initial fill state parameters and penetration metrics. This model will form the core of an inverse analysis framework to back-calculate pre-disturbance properties from a single test profile.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, minimally intrusive verification protocol for performance-based design of granular fills. It will provide a quantitative link between in-situ test results, construction quality, and foundational dynamic performance, moving beyond empirical correlations. The resulting framework will enable real-time feedback for compaction adjustment, reducing uncertainty in critical geotechnical applications.
proposals.novelty: This approach reframes in-situ test disturbance from a measurement error to a calibrated signal containing information about the initial soil fabric. The closed-loop, inverse methodology for translating a post-disturbance measurement into a pre-disturbance property and a construction recommendation is a significant departure from current practice.

proposals.title: Field Validation of Simplified Liquefaction Triggering Equations for Gravelly Soils Using Full-Scale Seismic Case Histories
proposals.motivation: Simplified liquefaction evaluation procedures, primarily calibrated for sandy soils, are often applied to gravelly soils despite significant differences in particle size, permeability, and dilatancy. This practice introduces uncertainty in seismic hazard assessment for infrastructure on or in gravelly alluvial deposits, as evidenced by recent case histories from the 2012 Emilia and 2020 earthquakes. A rigorous, large-scale validation against field performance is needed to assess the reliability and potential bias of these methods for gravels.
proposals.hypothesis: That recently proposed simplified liquefaction triggering equations, specifically those incorporating shear wave velocity (Vs) and cone penetration test (CPT) metrics adjusted for gravelly soils, will show statistically significant predictive improvement over conventional sandy-soil-based methods when tested against a compiled database of well-documented field case histories.
proposals.method: The research will compile a new, comprehensive database of field case histories from seismic events (e.g., 2012 Emilia, 2020 events) where gravelly soil performance is documented. For each site, in-situ test data (CPT, SPT, Vs) and seismic demand (peak ground acceleration, magnitude) will be used with both conventional (e.g., Boulanger & Idriss, 2014) and recently proposed gravel-adjusted equations to compute factor of safety against liquefaction. The binary outcome (liquefied/not liquefied) from field evidence will be compared to predictions using receiver operating characteristic (ROC) analysis and accuracy metrics. The statistical significance of differences in predictive performance will be tested.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative, evidence-based assessment of the predictive accuracy of simplified procedures for gravelly soils, directly informing geotechnical engineering practice. It will produce a publicly available, curated database of gravelly soil case histories for future research. The results will clarify whether existing adjustments are sufficient or if new, gravel-specific frameworks are required.
proposals.novelty: While case history compilations exist, this study focuses specifically on validating emerging *gravel-adjusted* equations against a targeted database of gravel-dominated sites, moving beyond anecdotal comparison to systematic, quantitative validation of these newer models.

proposals.title: A Probabilistic Framework for Seismic Liquefaction Hazard Mapping in Gravelly Alluvial Plains
proposals.motivation: Current regional seismic hazard maps, which inform building codes and risk assessments, often neglect or inadequately model liquefaction hazard in gravelly soils. This is a critical gap, as post-earthquake investigations (e.g., 2012 Emilia, 2020 events) and your own case-history studies confirm that clean and silty gravels can liquefy, posing significant risk to infrastructure. Integrating a validated gravel liquefaction model into Probabilistic Seismic Hazard Analysis (PSHA) is therefore essential for accurate risk quantification in regions like the Po Plain, Taiwan, or the western United States.
proposals.hypothesis: Incorporating a gravel-specific liquefaction triggering model, calibrated against in-situ test data (e.g., SPT, CPT) from liquefied sites, into a PSHA framework will produce significantly different and more reliable regional liquefaction hazard maps for gravelly alluvial deposits compared to maps derived from sand-centric procedures.
proposals.method: The research will employ a modular PSHA workflow. First, your existing gravel liquefaction model (based on case histories and in-situ tests) will be formalized as a probabilistic fragility function. Second, a logic tree will be constructed to capture epistemic uncertainties in seismic source characterization, ground motion prediction, and the gravel model itself. Third, using open-source PSHA software (e.g., OpenQuake), we will compute the annual probability of exceeding critical liquefaction potential indices across a test region (e.g., the Emilia-Romagna alluvial plain). The analysis will use high-resolution geotechnical unit maps to spatially distribute the gravel model.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first publicly available, physics-informed probabilistic liquefaction hazard maps explicitly for gravelly terrains. It will provide a transferable methodology for updating national seismic hazard models and directly inform site-specific investigation requirements and regional mitigation priorities.
proposals.novelty: While PSHA for sandy soils is established, the novel integration of a field-calibrated gravel liquefaction model into a full probabilistic hazard framework, moving beyond deterministic or index-based approaches, represents a significant advancement for regional risk assessment in coarse-grained soils.

proposals.title: A Novel Ground Improvement Technique: Controlled Liquefaction for Enhanced Grout Injection in Loose Silty Sands
proposals.motivation: Liquefaction of loose, saturated silty sands remains a major geotechnical hazard, as evidenced by extensive damage in recent events like the 2012 Emilia earthquake. Traditional ground improvement techniques, such as permeation grouting, often struggle with uniformity in these soils due to inherent heterogeneity and filtration. A method to improve grout distribution would significantly enhance the seismic resilience of infrastructure.
proposals.hypothesis: Inducing a controlled, limited liquefaction event in loose silty sands prior to grout injection will temporarily increase soil permeability and reduce effective stress, enabling more uniform and extensive penetration of low-viscosity colloidal silica or microfine cement grouts.
proposals.method: The research will combine field-scale physical modeling and in-situ testing. A large-scale laminar box or calibration chamber will be filled with a characterized silty sand. Controlled liquefaction will be triggered via a base shaking table or controlled blasting. Concurrently, grout will be injected through a designed array of injection points. The process will be monitored using embedded pore pressure transducers and shear wave velocity sensors. Post-treatment, the improved soil mass will be evaluated using cone penetration tests (CPT) and shear wave velocity profiles to generate updated liquefaction resistance curves, comparing them to pre-treatment baseline curves.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a first-of-its-kind framework for a novel ground improvement methodology that actively uses liquefaction for benefit. It will yield a dataset linking controlled liquefaction parameters to final grout distribution and soil densification, offering a new design approach for treating problematic loose silty sands.
proposals.novelty: While liquefaction is universally treated as a hazard to mitigate, this proposal inverts the paradigm by using it as a controlled tool to enable improvement. The integration of real-time geophysical monitoring with grouting during a triggered pore pressure response is a significant departure from conventional sequential ground improvement practices.

proposals.title: Re-evaluating the Role of Fines Plasticity in the Cyclic Liquefaction Resistance of Gravelly Soils: A Field Performance-Based Study
proposals.motivation: Current simplified liquefaction evaluation procedures, such as the Chinese Criteria (GB 50011) and adaptations of the Seed-Idriss method, often treat all fines below a plasticity index (PI) threshold as 'non-plastic' and assign them a similar correction. This approach is challenged by field case histories from events like the 2012 Emilia earthquakes, where liquefaction occurred in soils with plastic fines. The assumption that plasticity is irrelevant for seismic loading in gravelly soils requires rigorous testing against field performance data.
proposals.hypothesis: The cyclic liquefaction resistance of gravelly soils is not solely a function of fines content but is significantly influenced by the plasticity of the fines fraction, even at lower PI values typically considered 'non-plastic' (e.g., PI < 7). For certain seismic loading characteristics, plastic fines may provide a distinct, measurable increase in resistance compared to non-plastic fines of similar gradation.
proposals.method: The research will employ a field case history approach, expanding the database from recent papers on the 2012 Emilia and 2020 events. For each case history site, in-situ test data (primarily CPT and SPT) will be correlated with detailed laboratory index testing (Atterberg limits) on recovered samples to establish fines content and plasticity. Cyclic resistance ratios (CRR) will be derived from the case histories using established back-analysis methods. Statistical analysis and machine learning techniques (e.g., logistic regression and Random Forest classifiers) will be used to develop probabilistic curves relating CRR to normalized cone tip resistance (qt1Ncs), fines content (FC), and plasticity index (PI), explicitly testing the significance of the PI parameter.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a refined, data-driven framework for assessing liquefaction in gravelly soils that incorporates fines plasticity. It will deliver updated correlation curves for use with CPT and SPT that can improve the accuracy of hazard maps and engineering designs. The findings will directly inform potential revisions to building code provisions regarding the treatment of fines in liquefaction evaluation.
proposals.novelty: This proposal directly challenges the conventional binary (plastic/non-plastic) treatment of fines in liquefaction assessment by treating plasticity as a continuous variable. It leverages recent, well-documented case histories in gravelly soils, a material where the role of fines plasticity is less defined than in sandy soils, to test this hypothesis using advanced data analysis techniques.

proposals.title: A Microstructure-Integrated Framework for Predicting Flow Liquefaction in Bioclastic Sands: Cyclic Triaxial Testing and Micro-CT Validation
proposals.motivation: Offshore energy and infrastructure increasingly rely on foundations in calcareous, bioclastic sands, which exhibit complex seismic responses poorly captured by models for silica sands. Pre-2026 practice often treats liquefaction as binary, but the transitional behavior from cyclic mobility to flow liquefaction in these crushable, angular sands under high confinement is critical for risk assessment. Existing methods, like SPT/CPT-based curves, are largely empirical and calibrated for terrestrial silica sands, leaving a significant gap for marine deposits.
proposals.hypothesis: The transition from stable cyclic deformation to flow liquefaction in bioclastic sands is governed by a quantifiable interaction between bioclast content/intraparticle porosity, which controls particle crushing and pore pressure generation, and the initial in-situ fabric under site-specific confinement and drainage conditions.
proposals.method: We will perform a series of stress-controlled cyclic triaxial tests on reconstituted and frozen-tube samples of bioclastic sands with varying bioclast content (0-100%), simulating a range of confining pressures relevant to offshore depths (50-300 kPa). High-resolution pore pressure and strain measurements will map the complete failure envelope. Concurrently, micro-CT imaging of pre- and post-test specimens will quantify fabric evolution, particle breakage, and pore structure changes. Data will be used to calibrate and validate a constitutive model (e.g., a modified SANISAND framework) that explicitly incorporates a fabric tensor and a crushing-dependent critical state line.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, microstructure-informed constitutive model that predicts strain accumulation and pore pressure rise for bioclastic sands under complex seismic loading. It will provide a physics-based alternative to purely empirical liquefaction triggering curves, directly improving seismic hazard analysis for offshore foundations. The generated database of cyclic response and correlated micro-CT imagery will be a unique public resource.
proposals.novelty: The research moves beyond binary liquefaction assessment by systematically characterizing the *transitional* failure envelope, uniquely linking macroscopic cyclic response to quantified, 3D microstructural changes (including intraparticle porosity) specific to bioclastic sands, a material class underrepresented in pre-2026 models.

proposals.title: Liquefaction Susceptibility of Deep, Bioclast-Rich Marine Sands: Reconciling Field Case Histories with Advanced In Situ Testing
proposals.motivation: Current liquefaction assessment procedures, largely based on case histories in Holocene alluvial sands, may not be directly applicable to deeper, aged, and bioclastic marine deposits. The potential for such materials to liquefy under strong shaking is a critical uncertainty for seismic hazard assessment in coastal and offshore regions, including areas of planned infrastructure development. This gap is highlighted by the scarcity of well-documented case histories for these soil types in databases like those from the 2012 Emilia earthquake.
proposals.hypothesis: Deep (>15m), aged marine sands with significant bioclast (shell fragment) content possess a higher cyclic resistance than predicted by standard methods for clean sands, due to effects of cementation, aging, and particle shape, but may still liquefy under sufficiently strong shaking or altered drainage paths.
proposals.method: The research will employ a field-based approach, integrating Cone Penetration Test (CPT) and seismic cone (SCPT) soundings at carefully selected sites with known deep, bioclastic sand strata, building on Rollins' expertise. In-situ cyclic stress ratios will be estimated from shear wave velocity (Vs) profiles. This will be complemented by targeted borehole sampling and laboratory cyclic triaxial testing on high-quality frozen samples to measure cyclic resistance. The study will develop site-specific liquefaction triggering curves (CRR vs. (N1)60cs or qc1Ncs) and compare them to existing empirical curves for gravelly and clean sands.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce one of the first field-calibrated datasets on the liquefaction resistance of deep, bioclastic marine sands. It will provide practical guidance for geotechnical engineers on whether and how to adjust standard liquefaction evaluation procedures for these challenging soils. The findings will directly inform seismic microzonation and risk assessment for coastal plains and nearshore environments.
proposals.novelty: While the role of fines and aging is studied, the combined effect of significant bioclast content, deep burial, and marine depositional environment on liquefaction triggering is poorly quantified. This proposal moves beyond the well-studied alluvial settings (e.g., Po Plain) to systematically test a geological context often assumed to be non-liquefiable.

proposals.title: Grain-Scale DEM Investigation of Stratigraphic Discontinuities as Nucleation Points for Sand Boil Genesis During Seismic Liquefaction
proposals.motivation: Field case histories, including those from the 2012 Emilia and 2020 earthquakes, show that sand boil ejecta often have complex particle-size distributions, suggesting sourcing from multiple soil layers. However, standard liquefaction assessment methods treat source layers as homogeneous, ignoring the role of stratigraphic heterogeneity. A fundamental understanding of how discontinuous silt and sand lenses influence the initiation and composition of sand boils is lacking, limiting the interpretation of field observations for back-analysis.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that stratigraphic interfaces and layer discontinuities act as primary nucleation points for sand boil initiation during liquefaction, and that the resulting ejecta composition is a direct result of grain-scale mixing from adjacent, distinct soil layers, rather than from a single homogeneous source.
proposals.method: We will employ the Discrete Element Method (DEM) using the open-source YADE framework or PFC3D to construct a virtual, heterogeneous stratigraphy with discontinuous lenses of silt and sand within a gravelly soil matrix. Grains will be modeled as deformable spheres with contact laws (e.g., Hertz-Mindlin with rolling resistance) calibrated to pre-2026 cyclic simple shear test data. Seismic loading will be applied via a validated stress-controlled boundary, and the simulation will track the displacement and mixing of uniquely tagged particles from different layers. The analysis will focus on pore pressure localization, shear band formation, and the upward transport of mixed materials.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first grain-scale, mechanistic model of sand boil genesis from heterogeneous stratigraphy, directly linking field-observed ejecta composition to subsurface layer geometry. It will deliver a new conceptual framework for interpreting sand boils as indicators of subsurface heterogeneity, improving the forensic analysis of liquefied sites. The results will inform the development of next-generation assessment methods that account for stratigraphic complexity.
proposals.novelty: While DEM has been used to study homogeneous soil liquefaction, its application to model the genesis of sand boils from explicitly discontinuous, layered stratigraphy under seismic loading is novel. This approach uniquely bridges the scale gap between field geology and grain-scale mechanics to answer a long-standing question about ejecta sourcing.

proposals.title: Discontinuity of Silty Sand Source Layers: A Multi-Method Investigation of Sand Boil Composition and Stratigraphic Heterogeneity
proposals.motivation: Current liquefaction assessment and back-analysis often assume a continuous, homogeneous source layer for sand ejecta. This assumption, rooted in simplified models, may misrepresent the true stratigraphic complexity of alluvial deposits, such as those in the Po Plain. Understanding whether sand boils originate from a single layer or mix material from multiple thin, discontinuous lenses is critical for accurate post-liquefaction site characterization and for refining empirical liquefaction triggering curves, particularly for problematic silty sands and gravelly soils.
proposals.hypothesis: The silty sand source layer for earthquake-induced sand boils is not homogeneous and continuous; instead, sand boils are composed of a mixture of materials sourced from multiple, thin, and laterally discontinuous stratigraphic lenses.
proposals.method: We will perform detailed in-situ testing at well-documented liquefaction sites from the 2012 Emilia and 2020 earthquake sequences. The approach integrates high-resolution cone penetration tests (CPTu) with seismic dilatometer (SDMT) profiles to identify thin-layer discontinuities. Adjacent to sand boils, we will conduct carefully executed trenching and continuous tube sampling. Granulometric, mineralogical, and micromorphological analyses of the stratigraphic layers will be quantitatively compared to the ejecta material using principal component analysis (PCA) to statistically discriminate between a single source and a mixed provenance.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a field-validated, geotechnical framework for assessing source layer heterogeneity, moving beyond the homogeneous layer assumption. It will deliver a revised protocol for post-liquefaction field investigation and sampling. The findings will directly inform the selection of representative soil parameters for back-calculating field case histories used in liquefaction triggering models for silty and gravelly soils.
proposals.novelty: While stratigraphic complexity is acknowledged, this study directly tests the homogeneity assumption of the source layer by statistically linking in-situ stratigraphy to ejecta composition, a systematic approach not commonly applied in standard post-liquefaction investigations. It uniquely combines high-resolution penetration testing with forensic soil analysis specifically for this hypothesis.

proposals.title: A Geologically-Informed Network Resilience Model: Linking Petrographically-Defined Liquefaction Susceptibility of Quaternary Alluvial Plains to Port System Disruption
proposals.motivation: Major port networks are critical infrastructure hubs built on seismically active, liquefaction-prone coastal alluvial plains, such as the Po Plain. Current liquefaction susceptibility maps often rely on simplified index properties, lacking the sedimentological detail needed for regional-scale, unit-based vulnerability assessment. This gap hinders the development of seismic resilience plans that account for the shared subsurface geology connecting multiple port facilities within a network.
proposals.hypothesis: The integration of a petrographic correlation method for defining regional geological units with distinct liquefaction potential, into a port network interdependency model, will reveal critical, geology-controlled bottlenecks in system functionality post-earthquake that are not identified by conventional, site-specific assessments.
proposals.method: The research will first apply the petrographic correlation method, as used in studies of the Po Plain sequences, to create a regional GIS layer of Quaternary geological units with characterized sedimentology (e.g., silt content, depositional environment). Each unit will be assigned a liquefaction susceptibility rating based on correlated CPTu data and case histories from prior work. This layer will be spatially joined to the nodes (berths, terminals) and edges (roads, rails) of a selected port network. Using a graph-based network model (e.g., in Python with NetworkX), seismic scenarios will simulate disruption propagation where nodes/edges underlain by high-susceptibility units fail, analyzing cascading impacts on cargo flow and identifying critical paths.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, geospatially explicit framework for assessing port network resilience that directly grounds infrastructure vulnerability in subsurface geology. It will deliver a methodology to prioritize ground improvement and retrofit investments based on protecting network-wide functionality, rather than individual asset performance. The resulting model will be a decision-support tool for regional infrastructure planners.
proposals.novelty: The fusion of detailed, petrographically-defined geological units—a scale typically used for stratigraphic analysis—with infrastructure network interdependency modeling is a novel cross-disciplinary approach. It moves beyond point-based liquefaction assessment to model regional disruption based on shared geological vulnerability, which is not a standard practice in pre-2026 port risk studies.

proposals.title: A Petrographic Correlation Framework for Regional Liquefaction Susceptibility Mapping of Silty Alluvial Sequences in Coastal Plains
proposals.motivation: Liquefaction susceptibility assessments for silty soils remain challenging due to their transitional behavior and spatial variability. Existing methods, often reliant on discrete in-situ tests, struggle to provide continuous regional-scale maps, particularly in complex Quaternary marine-continental sequences common to many coastal alluvial plains. This gap hinders seismic risk mitigation for critical infrastructure in these densely populated areas.
proposals.hypothesis: The petrographic and sedimentological characteristics (e.g., mineralogy, microfossil content, grain shape) of silty units, derived from a limited set of boreholes, can be systematically correlated across a region to delineate spatially continuous units with consistent liquefaction resistance, as calibrated against field performance case histories.
proposals.method: The method extends the petrographic correlation approach from Rollins' prior Po Plain work. First, we will compile a regional database of borehole logs, CPTu soundings, and shear wave velocity profiles from a target coastal plain (e.g., the Venetian or Romagna coast, Italy). Core samples from key silt layers will undergo detailed petrographic analysis (thin-section microscopy, X-ray diffraction, micropaleontology). Using hierarchical cluster analysis and discriminant analysis, we will establish diagnostic fingerprints for distinct silty facies. These facies will be correlated spatially using geostatistical kriging, constrained by depositional environment maps. The resulting regional 3D model will be validated by comparing predicted facies at documented liquefaction/no-liquefaction sites from historical earthquakes against field-derived liquefaction triggering curves.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, geologically-grounded regional liquefaction susceptibility map for silty soils, moving beyond point-based assessments. It will deliver a transferable petrographic correlation framework applicable to other alluvial-coastal settings. The research will also advance the understanding of how depositional environment and diagenesis control the cyclic resistance of silty sediments.
proposals.novelty: While petrographic methods are established in stratigraphy, their systematic application to predict a geotechnical property (liquefaction susceptibility) across a region represents a novel synthesis.
