This approach integrates geological history directly into engineering vulnerability mapping for silts, a significant advance over purely index-based or penetration-based correlations.

proposals.title: SDMT-Constrained Calibration of a Pore Pressure Generation Model for Performance-Based Optimization of Ground Improvement in Silt-Rich Soils
proposals.motivation: Current empirical liquefaction assessment methods, particularly for intermediate soils like silty sands and sandy silts, are often conservative and lack a direct link to the dynamic pore pressure response that governs liquefaction triggering. This leads to potentially inefficient ground improvement designs. The Seismic Dilatometer (SDMT) provides a rich, in-situ dataset of parameters (e.g., material index I_D, shear wave velocity V_s, horizontal stress index K_D) sensitive to soil state and stress history, offering an untapped resource for calibrating advanced constitutive models.
proposals.hypothesis: That SDMT-derived parameters (I_D, K_D, V_s) can be used to robustly calibrate a constitutive model (e.g., a stress-ratio controlled, cyclic-strain based pore pressure generation model) for silt-rich soils, and that this calibrated model can then be used to optimize ground improvement schemes by directly targeting the control of excess pore pressure ratio (r_u) under design seismic loading.
proposals.method: The research will compile a database of well-documented liquefaction case histories, focusing on sites with silt-rich layers and pre-event SDMT soundings. A cyclic pore pressure generation model (e.g., within the PM4Silt or a similar bounding surface plasticity framework) will be implemented in finite difference software. Key model parameters will be statistically correlated to SDMT indices (I_D, K_D) using regression and Bayesian updating techniques. The calibrated model will be validated against field performance. Finally, a parametric study using the model will optimize ground improvement (e.g., stone column) spacing and stiffness by minimizing the computed r_u to meet a performance target (e.g., r_u < 0.8).
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, SDMT-informed methodology for calibrating advanced constitutive models, moving beyond empirical correlations. It will produce a performance-based design framework that directly links ground improvement geometry to a physics-based pore pressure response metric, enabling more efficient and reliable liquefaction mitigation designs tailored to site-specific soil conditions.
proposals.novelty: The direct use of pre-event SDMT data to calibrate a dynamic pore pressure model for constitutive analysis, and the subsequent application of that model to explicitly optimize ground improvement design parameters based on controlling the computed excess pore pressure ratio, represents a significant departure from current empirical or simplified practice.

proposals.title: Numerical Modeling of Pore Pressure Dynamics in a Confined Silty Sand Layer during the 2016 Central Italy Earthquake Sequence
proposals.motivation: Liquefaction assessment in gravelly soils and clean sands is well-studied, but the behavior of intermediate soils like silty sands remains a critical uncertainty. The 2016 Central Italy earthquakes, which induced liquefaction in the Po Plain, provide a documented case history with a distinct, continuous 2-4.5 m thick silty sand layer. Analyzing pore pressure dynamics in this layer is essential to improve predictive models for mixed soils common in alluvial and coastal settings.
proposals.hypothesis: The pore pressure generation and dissipation in the silty sand layer during the 2016 seismic sequence were governed by a combination of its fines content, permeability, and the inter-layer drainage conditions imposed by the surrounding strata, leading to a delayed or partial liquefaction response compared to cleaner sands.
proposals.method: We will develop a 1D effective-stress site response model using a platform like OpenSees or DEEPSOIL. The soil column will be constructed using the detailed stratigraphy and in-situ test data (CPTu, SPT) from the Emilia/2012 and 2016 event databases. The silty sand layer will be modeled using a constitutive model (e.g., PM4Silt or a modified Drucker-Prager formulation) calibrated to its cyclic resistance from laboratory data. We will input the recorded 2016 accelerograms and explicitly model pore pressure generation and dissipation using coupled u-p formulations to analyze buildup rates and post-shaking dissipation.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a validated numerical case study quantifying the pore pressure response of a natural silty sand deposit to a real earthquake sequence. It will contribute a calibrated model and parameters for similar intermediate soils, directly informing the refinement of empirical CPT/SPT-based liquefaction triggering procedures for soils with significant fines.
proposals.novelty: While many studies model idealized layers, this proposal focuses on a real, well-characterized silty sand stratum from a major earthquake, integrating detailed pre-2026 in-situ data and advanced constitutive models specifically tailored for silts to dissect its unique pressure generation and dissipation mechanics.

proposals.title: Probabilistic Deconvolution of Liquefied Soil Mixtures Using Detrital Zircon Geochronology and Heavy Mineral Fingerprints
proposals.motivation: Current forensic analysis of earthquake-induced liquefaction features relies on qualitative matching of soil composition, which struggles to uniquely identify and quantify contributions from multiple, interbedded source layers. This limits our ability to reconstruct the precise subsurface flow paths and layer susceptibility, which is critical for validating and improving liquefaction triggering models, especially in complex gravelly soils. A more quantitative, probabilistic framework is needed to move beyond simple visual correlation.
proposals.hypothesis: The sediment mixture extruded into a liquefaction feature can be statistically deconvolved into its contributing source layers by treating diagnostic mineralogical and geochronological data as high-dimensional markers, analogous to genetic markers in forensic DNA mixture analysis. The proportional contribution of each source layer can be estimated using a probabilistic model.
proposals.method: We will collect paired samples from liquefaction ejecta and adjacent borehole cores/CPT-identified soil layers at well-documented sites (e.g., from the 2012 Emilia or 2020 earthquake databases). Samples will be analyzed for heavy mineral assemblages via automated SEM-EDS and for detrital zircon U-Pb age spectra. We will adapt the core algorithms of probabilistic genotyping software (e.g., EuroForMix's continuous model) to handle our continuous geochemical/geochronological data. The model will use Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) sampling to estimate the number of contributors and their mixing proportions by comparing the likelihood of the mixture's 'fingerprint' against potential source layer combinations.
proposals.contribution: This research will deliver a novel, quantitative methodology for forensic liquefaction analysis, providing a statistically rigorous estimate of source layer contributions. It will advance the state-of-practice from qualitative matching to probabilistic deconvolution, offering a new tool to calibrate in-situ test-based liquefaction triggering curves against actual subsurface failure mechanisms. The framework will be particularly valuable for assessing liquefaction in stratified gravelly soils where traditional CPT interpretations are challenging.
proposals.novelty: The direct adaptation of forensic DNA mixture deconvolution algorithms and statistical frameworks to a geotechnical sediment sourcing problem is a fundamentally novel cross-disciplinary analogy. While detrital zircon analysis is used for provenance, its application within a formal probabilistic mixture model to solve an engineering forensic problem is unprecedented.

proposals.title: Fingerprinting Liquefaction Source Layers in Gravelly Alluvial Plains: Integrating Sedimentary Provenance Analysis with In-Situ Geotechnical Data
proposals.motivation: Current liquefaction assessment in gravelly soils relies heavily on in-situ tests (e.g., CPT) and empirical curves, which can be highly uncertain due to material heterogeneity and the challenge of identifying the specific subsurface layer that actually liquefies. This uncertainty hampers the development of reliable, case-history-based models for gravels. A more precise method to correlate surface ejecta with their source stratigraphic units is needed to refine these models and improve hazard maps.
proposals.hypothesis: Heavy mineral assemblages and detrital zircon U-Pb age spectra from liquefaction ejecta are diagnostic of specific Pleistocene-Holocene alluvial depositional units within a basin, allowing for unambiguous correlation of ejecta to their subsurface source layers, thereby constraining the geotechnical properties of the layers that actually liquefy.
proposals.method: The research will apply established sedimentary provenance techniques to case histories from well-documented sites (e.g., the 2012 Emilia and 2020 earthquake sequences). For each site, heavy mineral analysis via optical microscopy and SEM-EDS will be conducted on ejecta samples and on targeted borehole samples from candidate source layers identified via CPT logs. Detrital zircon geochronology will be performed using laser ablation-inductively coupled plasma-mass spectrometry (LA-ICP-MS) to obtain U-Pb age spectra. Provenance fingerprints will be statistically compared using multidimensional scaling (MDS) and likelihood-based correlation algorithms to establish genetic links between ejecta and subsurface layers.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, geology-based framework to definitively link surface manifestations of liquefaction to their source depth, reducing uncertainty in case history databases. It will yield a refined set of in-situ test (CPT/SPT) data points specifically tied to verified liquefiable layers in gravelly soils, enabling more accurate calibration of empirical assessment curves. The methodology will establish provenance analysis as a new tool for paleoliquefaction studies.
proposals.novelty: While provenance analysis is standard in sedimentology, its application to solve a core uncertainty in geotechnical earthquake engineering—specifically identifying the *source layer* of liquefaction ejecta—is novel. This approach moves beyond correlating layers based solely on grain size or SPT/CPT data, using a genetic, mineralogical fingerprint instead.

proposals.title: Geostatistical Kriging of Mineralogical Composition for Probabilistic Liquefaction Susceptibility Mapping in Quaternary Alluvial Plains
proposals.motivation: Current liquefaction susceptibility assessments often treat soil units as homogeneous, ignoring intrinsic compositional heterogeneity that critically influences cyclic resistance. This is particularly relevant in gravelly soils and silty sands of alluvial plains, like the Po Valley, where case histories show variable performance. A method to spatially predict this variability from sparse data is needed to improve hazard mapping accuracy.
proposals.hypothesis: The spatial continuity of critical mineralogical ratios (e.g., litharenite/quartz) can be modeled as a regionalized variable using geostatistics, and this spatially explicit composition model can predict zones of elevated liquefaction susceptibility within a nominally uniform geologic unit more reliably than uniform unit-based methods.
proposals.method: The research will fuse geostatistics with the inverted soil composition hypothesis. Using a database of borehole logs, CPT soundings, and mineralogical data from sites like the 2012 Emilia earthquake, we will treat mineralogical ratios as primary variables. Ordinary kriging and co-kriging (using CPT parameters as covariates) will interpolate these ratios across 3D space. The interpolated composition fields will then inform probabilistic liquefaction triggering analyses using established CPT-based methods (e.g., Boulanger & Idriss, 2014) to generate spatial hazard maps.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, spatially explicit framework for liquefaction susceptibility assessment that accounts for intrinsic compositional heterogeneity. It will provide a methodology to generate probabilistic hazard maps from sparse mineralogical data, directly integrating geological provenance with geotechnical analysis. The resulting tool will be particularly valuable for critical infrastructure projects in complex alluvial settings.
proposals.novelty: The fusion of geostatistical mineralogical kriging with liquefaction triggering models is a novel, spatially explicit approach. It moves beyond treating soil units as uniform by directly modeling and mapping the continuous variability of a key intrinsic susceptibility control.

proposals.title: Geostatistical Kriging for 3D Stratigraphic Correlation of Liquefiable Silty Sand Units in Alluvial Plains
proposals.motivation: Current liquefaction hazard assessments rely heavily on discrete in-situ tests (e.g., CPT, SPT) at specific points, creating uncertainty in the lateral and vertical continuity of critical liquefiable layers. This spatial uncertainty is a major limitation for site characterization, particularly in heterogeneous alluvial deposits like the Po Plain, where Rollins' recent work is focused. A robust method to predict the 3D geometry of these units between boreholes would significantly improve the accuracy of liquefaction triggering and consequence analyses.
proposals.hypothesis: Ordinary kriging and indicator kriging, using geotechnical and geological data from boreholes and cone penetration tests (CPTu), can accurately model the spatial continuity and probability of occurrence of liquefiable silty sand units in Quaternary alluvial settings.
proposals.method: The research will compile a 3D database from documented case histories (e.g., 2012 Emilia, 2020 events) containing CPTu tip resistance (qt), sleeve friction (fs), and soil behavior type index (Ic) as primary variables, alongside borehole stratigraphy. Variogram analysis will quantify spatial autocorrelation of key parameters (e.g., Ic) within the target unit. Ordinary kriging will interpolate continuous property fields, while indicator kriging, using a threshold Ic value to define 'liquefiable silty sand', will generate 3D probability models of unit presence. Validation will be performed using hold-out boreholes and comparing kriged predictions to actual encountered stratigraphy.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal, quantitative framework for correlating liquefiable strata between investigation points, moving beyond qualitative cross-sections. It will deliver a methodology to generate 3D probabilistic models of liquefiable unit geometry, directly informing more reliable spatial liquefaction hazard maps and finite element model inputs for performance-based earthquake engineering.
proposals.novelty: While kriging is standard in mining and hydrogeology, its systematic application to correlate specific, engineering-defined liquefiable soil units—integrating high-resolution CPTu data with geological interpretation—is novel in geotechnical earthquake engineering. This bridges a gap between detailed site-specific testing and regional geological modeling.

proposals.title: A Machine Learning Framework for Forensic Stratigraphy and Source Layer Attribution of Liquefaction Ejecta
proposals.motivation: Current liquefaction assessments, particularly in gravelly soils, rely on in-situ tests (CPTu, DMT, Vs) to predict susceptibility but lack a physical validation linking surface ejecta to specific subsurface source layers. This gap limits the forensic analysis of liquefaction events and the refinement of predictive models. A method to definitively attribute sand boil ejecta to their stratigraphic origin would transform post-event investigations and improve the spatial accuracy of vulnerability maps.
proposals.hypothesis: Machine learning models trained on fused CPTu, DMT, and Vs data can generate high-resolution 3D stratigraphic models that, when combined with forensic signatures of ejecta, enable accurate inverse attribution of liquefied material to its precise subsurface source layer.
proposals.method: The approach integrates: 1) Fusion of CPTu, DMT, and Vs data using a Random Forest or Gradient Boosting model to predict high-resolution soil behavior type (SBT) and liquefaction susceptibility index profiles. 2) Forensic analysis of post-event ejecta using colorimetry, loss-on-ignition for organic content, and portable XRF for mineralogy to create a material signature. 3) An inverse matching algorithm (e.g., a k-NN or probabilistic model) to compare the ejecta signature against the ML-predicted stratigraphic model, identifying the most probable source layer based on multi-parameter similarity.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, closed-loop framework that physically validates liquefaction predictions through forensic evidence, moving beyond probabilistic assessment. It will provide a new tool for forensic geotechnical engineering to determine the depth and stratum of liquefaction, directly informing the revision of liquefaction triggering curves, especially for complex gravelly soils.
proposals.novelty: The research uniquely fuses multi-parameter in-situ data fusion via ML with forensic soil analysis for inverse source attribution, a synthesis not present in pre-2026 literature. It creates a tangible link between predicted stratigraphic models and physical ejecta, enabling ground-truthing of liquefaction models.

proposals.title: Forensic Provenance of Liquefaction Ejecta: Linking Sand Boils to Source Layers in Gravelly Soils Using Colorimetry and Organic Matter Analysis
proposals.motivation: Post-liquefaction forensic analysis currently relies heavily on in-situ tests (CPT, SPT) to characterize the liquefied layer, but these methods cannot definitively prove the origin of ejected sand boil material. This creates uncertainty in back-analyses of case histories, particularly in complex gravelly soils where stratigraphy is heterogeneous. A method to physically trace ejecta to its subsurface source would significantly improve the validation of liquefaction triggering and consequence assessments.
proposals.hypothesis: Sand boil ejecta possesses a distinct geochemical and physical signature (e.g., color, organic matter content/composition) that can be matched to its specific subsurface source layer through comparative forensic analysis, thereby providing direct physical evidence of the liquefaction source depth and layer composition.
proposals.method: The research will apply established forensic soil comparison techniques to well-documented liquefaction case histories (e.g., from the 2012 Emilia or 2020 earthquake databases). Paired samples of surface sand boils and subsurface soils from adjacent boreholes/CPT soundings will be collected. Analysis will include: 1) Quantitative colorimetry using a spectrophotometer to derive CIELAB color coordinates (L*, a*, b*); 2) Loss-on-ignition and potentially pyrolysis-gas chromatography/mass spectrometry (Py-GC/MS) to characterize organic matter type and content. Statistical matching (e.g., principal component analysis, discriminant analysis) will be used to correlate ejecta signatures to specific depth intervals.
proposals.contribution: This work will develop a novel, direct physical tracing methodology for liquefaction studies, moving beyond inference. It will provide a new line of evidence to calibrate and verify interpretations from CPT/SPT-based liquefaction triggering procedures. The resulting protocols could become a standard for high-quality forensic liquefaction investigation, especially in critical infrastructure assessments.
proposals.novelty: While forensic soil techniques are mature in environmental and criminalistics fields, their systematic application to geotechnical earthquake engineering and liquefaction provenance is novel. This approach directly addresses the physical linkage problem between ejecta and source, a gap not filled by conventional penetration testing.

proposals.title: Non-invasive Imaging of Shallow Silty Sand Layers for Liquefaction Assessment Using Ambient Noise Seismic Interferometry
proposals.motivation: Accurate characterization of shallow, loose silty sand layers is critical for liquefaction hazard assessment, yet traditional invasive methods like cone penetration tests (CPT) provide only point measurements. Geophysical methods offer non-invasive imaging but often rely on active sources, which are costly and logistically challenging in urban environments. Ambient noise seismic interferometry (ANSI), which extracts subsurface information from background vibrations, presents a promising but underexplored alternative for high-resolution, cost-effective site characterization in liquefaction-prone areas.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that ambient noise seismic interferometry can reliably image the geometry (depth, thickness, and lateral continuity) of shallow, liquefiable silty sand layers by reconstructing surface waves and deriving shear-wave velocity (Vs) profiles, and that these Vs profiles will correlate strongly with in-situ penetration resistance (e.g., CPT qc) at validation sites.
proposals.method: The method will deploy dense arrays of vertical-component geophones at well-documented, liquefied sites from the 2012 Emilia and 2020 earthquake databases. We will apply seismic interferometry by cross-correlating long-duration ambient noise recordings between all sensor pairs to extract empirical Green's functions, primarily capturing surface waves. The dispersion curves of the extracted Rayleigh waves will be inverted using a laterally constrained inversion algorithm to produce 2D shear-wave velocity (Vs) sections. These Vs profiles will be quantitatively compared to co-located CPT soundings and borehole data to calibrate the relationship between Vs and soil liquefaction resistance.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, non-invasive protocol for imaging the geometry of critical liquefiable strata, directly addressing a key uncertainty in regional hazard maps. It will contribute a new field-data-based correlation between Vs profiles from ANSI and penetration-based liquefaction triggering curves for silty sands, enhancing the toolkit for performance-based earthquake engineering.
proposals.novelty: While ANSI is established in deep geophysics, its targeted application for high-resolution imaging of shallow, liquefiable silty sand layers—and its direct integration with a rich database of field case histories and penetration-based liquefaction assessments—is novel. This bridges a gap between geophysical imaging and geotechnical engineering practice for liquefaction evaluation.

proposals.title: A Probabilistic Framework for Coastal Liquefaction Hazard from Subduction Earthquakes: Integrating a Gravelly and Silty Sand Model into PSHA
proposals.motivation: Current probabilistic seismic hazard assessments (PSHAs) for subduction zones, which threaten vast coastal margins, often inadequately model liquefaction hazard, particularly for mixed and gravelly soils common in alluvial and coastal plains. The 2011 Tohoku and 2012 Emilia earthquakes demonstrated the severe consequences of widespread liquefaction in such deposits. A scalable, geologically-informed liquefaction model is needed to move from site-specific analysis to continental-scale risk assessment for critical infrastructure.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating a next-generation liquefaction triggering model, calibrated for silty sands and gravelly soils using in-situ tests (CPT, SPT) and case histories, into a PSHA framework will significantly improve the spatial accuracy and reliability of liquefaction probability estimates across heterogeneous coastal margins.
proposals.method: The method involves three steps: 1) Develop a regionally adaptable probabilistic liquefaction model building on the researcher's previous work, using Bayesian updating to incorporate case history data (e.g., Emilia 2012 database) and in-situ test curves for soils with fines and gravel content. 2) Spatially discretize target coastlines (e.g., Cascadia, Japan) using pre-2026 geotechnical and geological databases (e.g., USGS maps, borehole logs) to assign model parameters. 3) Implement the model within an open-source PSHA engine (e.g., OpenQuake), where ground motion fields from subduction ruptures drive the liquefaction calculation at each grid cell, producing hazard curves for liquefaction triggering.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first open-source, physics-informed framework for continental-scale liquefaction hazard assessment specifically tailored for subduction zone coastlines. It will deliver actionable hazard maps that quantify uncertainty and directly inform land-use planning and building code provisions for coastal communities. The integrated model will bridge the gap between detailed geotechnical engineering and regional seismology.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct integration of a advanced, soil-type-specific liquefaction model—validated for problematic gravelly and silty soils—into a full probabilistic seismic hazard workflow at a continental scale, moving beyond simplified, clean-sand-based correlations typically used in large-area assessments.

proposals.title: Inverse Estimation of Seismic Demand from Sand Boil Morphology and Ejecta Characteristics: A Field-Data-Driven Framework
proposals.motivation: Post-earthquake liquefaction assessments rely heavily on in-situ tests (e.g., CPT, SPT) to estimate the cyclic stress ratio (CSR) that caused liquefaction. However, these tests are expensive and logistically challenging immediately after an event. Sand boils, as ubiquitous surface manifestations, contain untapped quantitative information about the subsurface liquefaction process and the seismic shaking that triggered it. Developing a method to inversely constrain shaking intensity from sand boil characteristics would provide a rapid, low-cost forensic tool for seismic hazard analysis and model calibration.
proposals.hypothesis: The morphology (e.g., area, volume, distribution) and composition (e.g., grain size distribution, fines content) of erupted sand boil ejecta are systematically related to the peak ground acceleration (PGA) or cyclic stress ratio (CSR) experienced at the site, allowing for their use as inverse proxies for seismic demand.
proposals.method: The research will compile a new, detailed database from well-documented case histories (e.g., 2012 Emilia, 2020 events) pairing quantified sand boil metrics (from post-event surveys and satellite imagery) with independently estimated CSR/PGA from adjacent CPT/SPT profiles and ground motion models. A Bayesian inverse framework will be developed, treating the sand boil characteristics as observables. The forward model will link CSR to ejecta volume and composition via empirical relationships for volumetric strain and layer thickness, informed by pre-2026 constitutive models. Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) sampling will be used to infer posterior distributions of CSR/PGA, constrained by the field data.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, field-validated methodology for estimating seismic shaking intensity from geological evidence alone. It will deliver a practical protocol for forensic earthquake engineering and contribute a new dataset linking surface manifestations to subsurface response. The framework will also improve the interpretation of paleoliquefaction features for seismic hazard studies.
proposals.novelty: While sand boils are commonly used for liquefaction identification, using their physical characteristics to quantitatively and inversely solve for the causative shaking intensity is a significant departure from standard practice. This approach inverts the typical analysis flow, treating the surface effect as the primary data source to constrain the seismic input.

proposals.title: Grain-Scale Mechanisms of Sand Boil Initiation in Silty Sands: A Coupled DEM-Fluid Dynamics Investigation
proposals.motivation: Liquefaction-induced sand boils are a critical failure mechanism, yet their initiation from a micromechanical perspective, particularly in silty sands common in alluvial plains like the Po Valley, remains poorly understood. Current empirical methods, based on in-situ tests like CPT and case histories, lack a physics-based explanation for how fines content and fabric influence the localized instability leading to boiling. A grain-scale investigation is needed to bridge the gap between macroscopic field observations and the underlying particle-scale processes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that sand boil initiation in silty sands is governed by the formation of a localized, unstable force chain network and the development of connected porosity channels, which is critically sensitive to the spatial distribution of fines and the initial soil fabric.
proposals.method: We will employ 3D Discrete Element Method (DEM) using the open-source LIGGGHTS or YADE framework, modeling sand grains as spheres and silt as finer particles. A simplified coupled fluid scheme (e.g., a pore pressure diffusion model or a coarse-grained CFD-DEM coupling) will be implemented to simulate excess pore pressure generation and dissipation. Cyclic shear will be applied to a representative volume element to simulate undrained loading, with analysis focused on the evolution of contact force networks, fabric anisotropy, and the emergence of fluidization channels.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a first-principles, micromechanical model of sand boil initiation, directly linking fines content and fabric to post-liquefaction behavior. It will deliver quantitative metrics (e.g., critical fabric anisotropy, channel connectivity threshold) that can inform the interpretation of in-situ tests and improve the physical basis for liquefaction consequence assessments.
proposals.novelty: While DEM has been used for clean sand liquefaction, its application to model the complete process of sand boil initiation in silty sands, explicitly incorporating fines and a coupled fluid phase, represents a significant advance beyond pre-2026 continuum and empirical approaches.

proposals.title: A Stochastic Framework for Probabilistic Liquefaction Hazard Assessment of Heterogeneous Gravelly Alluvial Deposits
proposals.motivation: Current liquefaction assessment methods, particularly for complex gravelly soils, often rely on deterministic analyses of simplified, homogeneous soil profiles. This neglects the inherent spatial variability of natural alluvial deposits, such as those documented in the 2012 Emilia and 2020 earthquake case histories. This omission can lead to significant underestimation of both liquefaction potential and its spatial variability, resulting in non-conservative or inefficient engineering designs.
proposals.hypothesis: The probabilistic liquefaction hazard for a site is governed not only by the median soil properties but by the statistical distribution and spatial correlation of key parameters (e.g., sandy cap thickness, permeability). We hypothesize that certain realistic configurations of subsurface heterogeneity, which are not captured in deterministic analyses, will disproportionately control the site's overall performance and risk.
proposals.method: The framework integrates geostatistical simulation (e.g., Sequential Gaussian Simulation) with advanced numerical modeling (e.g., OpenSees or FLAC with PM4Sand/PM4Silt constitutive models). First, an ensemble of geologically plausible 2D/3D subsurface realizations will be generated, parameterized from CPT and SPT data from case history databases. Each realization will undergo fully-coupled nonlinear effective stress seismic site response analysis. The results will be aggregated to produce probabilistic hazard maps of liquefaction severity and fragility curves linking ground motion intensity to the probability of exceeding defined performance levels.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a practical, risk-informed assessment tool that explicitly quantifies uncertainty arising from subsurface heterogeneity. It will provide new insights into which geometric and hydraulic configurations in layered gravelly systems are most vulnerable, moving beyond index-based methods. The resulting fragility curves will directly support performance-based earthquake engineering for critical infrastructure on alluvial plains.
proposals.novelty: While stochastic methods exist in geotechnics, their coupling with high-fidelity numerical models for liquefaction assessment of gravels is novel. This approach moves beyond calibrating models to single case histories and instead uses the ensemble to directly derive design-oriented probabilistic outputs from first principles of spatial variability.

proposals.title: A Hierarchical, Energy-Based Model for Excess Pore Pressure Generation in Gravelly Soils: Explicit Incorporation of Soil State and Fabric via Advanced Laboratory Calibration
proposals.motivation: Current empirical liquefaction triggering curves, often based on simplified indices like (N1)60, exhibit significant scatter for gravelly soils and fail to account for the profound influence of soil fabric, stress history, and inherent anisotropy. This limits the reliability of liquefaction assessments in critical infrastructure projects and post-earthquake evaluations, as evidenced by field case histories from events like the 2012 Emilia and 2020 earthquakes. A physics-based framework that systematically integrates these state parameters is urgently needed to move beyond site-specific correlations.
proposals.hypothesis: Excess pore pressure ratio (ru) can be robustly predicted by a normalized dissipated energy function, where the relationship is scaled by modular, quantifiable functions of soil state (e.g., state parameter, OCR) and fabric (e.g., initial anisotropy, particle arrangement).
proposals.method: The research will first establish a core energy-based ru relationship from cyclic direct simple shear and triaxial tests on reconstituted specimens of sands and gravelly soils. Advanced laboratory techniques—including bender elements for shear wave velocity (Vs) measurement of fabric anisotropy and micro-CT scanning for quantitative soil structure indices—will be used to characterize fabric. These state and fabric metrics will then be incorporated as scaling parameters within a hierarchical Bayesian framework to calibrate the modular functions, explicitly treating uncertainty. The model will be validated against a newly compiled database of high-quality laboratory tests and well-documented field case histories, including those from the 2012 Emilia earthquake.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a mechanics-based predictive model for ru that significantly improves generalizability across soil types and depositional histories. It will provide a calibrated database linking advanced fabric measurements to cyclic response, offering a new paradigm for interpreting in-situ tests like CPT and Vs. The hierarchical Bayesian formulation will explicitly quantify prediction uncertainty, enhancing decision-making in engineering practice.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit, quantitative integration of directly measured fabric anisotropy and soil structure indices—obtained via non-destructive geophysical and imaging methods—into a scalable, energy-based pore pressure generation framework, moving beyond purely empirical or phenomenological models.

proposals.title: A Multivariate Framework for Predicting Pore Pressure Ratio in Gravelly Soils: Integrating In-Situ State, Fabric, and Stress History
proposals.motivation: Current empirical correlations for excess pore pressure ratio (r_u) during liquefaction, primarily based on penetration resistance (e.g., CPT, SPT), show significant scatter, especially for gravelly soils. This uncertainty compromises the reliability of liquefaction triggering assessments and post-event analyses, as evidenced by case histories from the 2012 Emilia and 2020 earthquakes. A more comprehensive model incorporating a broader set of soil state parameters is needed to improve predictive accuracy.
proposals.hypothesis: The generation of excess pore pressure in gravelly soils during seismic loading can be more accurately predicted by a multivariate model that integrates in-situ state parameters (e.g., relative density, confining stress), fabric metrics (e.g., fines content, gradation), and stress history, rather than by penetration resistance alone.
proposals.method: The research will leverage and expand Dr. Rollins's existing databases of liquefaction case histories, focusing on gravelly soil sites. Key soil parameters will be compiled or derived from in-situ tests (CPT, SPT, shear wave velocity) and laboratory data. A multivariate regression analysis, potentially employing machine learning algorithms like Random Forest or Gradient Boosting, will be used to develop a new predictive model for r_u. The model's input features will include normalized penetration resistance, soil behavior type index, overburden stress, fines content, and an estimate of pre-shaking static shear stress.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a new, empirically grounded correlation for r_u that directly accounts for soil fabric and state, moving beyond simplified SPT/CPT-based curves. It will provide a practical tool for geotechnical engineers to make more reliable predictions of pore pressure development in gravelly soils, thereby improving liquefaction hazard evaluation. The expanded database will also serve as a valuable resource for future research.
proposals.novelty: While existing models correlate r_u with penetration resistance, this proposal systematically integrates fabric parameters (e.g., fines content) and stress history into a unified predictive framework specifically calibrated for gravelly soils, a material type where traditional methods are less well-constrained.

proposals.title: A Physics-Constrained Causal Machine Learning Framework for Generalizing Liquefaction Assessment in Gravelly Soils
proposals.motivation: Current empirical liquefaction assessment methods, particularly for gravelly soils, rely heavily on case history databases that are geographically and geologically limited, leading to uncertain extrapolation. Pure machine learning models trained on these data can produce physically implausible predictions and fail to generalize to new settings. A hybrid approach that enforces physical laws while learning from data is needed to create robust, transferable models for critical infrastructure.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating geotechnical first principles as hard constraints into a neural network architecture will produce more physically plausible and generalizable liquefaction predictions than purely data-driven models, and causal inference can identify stable, non-spurious drivers of liquefaction across diverse sites.
proposals.method: We will develop a Physics-Informed Neural Network (PINN) where the loss function incorporates both data misfit and residuals from governing equations (e.g., stress-strain relationships, critical state theory). A separate causal discovery module, using algorithms like PC or FCI, will analyze the fused database (including Rollins' gravelly soil case histories) to infer a Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) of causal relationships. An adaptive sampling strategy, based on Bayesian optimization of model uncertainty, will then identify optimal locations for future cone penetration tests (CPT) to maximally reduce prediction variance.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, open-source computational framework for liquefaction assessment that is demonstrably more generalizable than current methods. It will provide a causal interpretation of liquefaction triggers in gravelly soils and a systematic protocol for guiding cost-effective field investigations to improve model robustness iteratively.
proposals.novelty: The integration of physics as hard constraints (not just soft regularization) within a causal ML framework for geotechnical engineering is novel. Furthermore, closing the loop by using model uncertainty to guide physical testing (adaptive sampling) for model improvement represents a significant advance over static empirical correlations.

proposals.title: A Machine Learning Framework for Predicting Liquefaction Potential in Gravelly Soils Using Integrated CPT and Compositional Data
proposals.motivation: Current simplified liquefaction evaluation procedures, primarily developed for sandy soils, show significant uncertainty when applied to gravelly soils due to their complex composition and the difficulty of obtaining undisturbed samples. This gap leads to unreliable hazard assessments for critical infrastructure founded on or in these materials. Leveraging the growing database of well-documented case histories, including those from the 2012 Emilia and 2020 earthquakes, provides a timely opportunity to develop more robust predictive models.
proposals.hypothesis: Machine learning models trained on integrated in-situ test data (e.g., CPT) and soil composition indices (e.g., fines content, gravel percentage) can predict the liquefaction potential of gravelly soils with greater accuracy than conventional, composition-agnostic methods.
proposals.method: We will compile a comprehensive database from published case histories, including CPT soundings, soil composition (grain size distribution, fines content), and performance (liquefied/non-liquefied). Feature engineering will create inputs such as normalized CPT resistance, composition-based correction factors, and depth parameters. We will train and compare several pre-2026 ML algorithms, including Random Forests, Gradient Boosting Machines (e.g., XGBoost), and simple neural networks, using k-fold cross-validation. Model performance will be benchmarked against established empirical curves and the Seed-Idriss simplified procedure.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a transparent, data-driven model that explicitly quantifies the influence of gravel and fines content on liquefaction resistance. It will provide a practical tool for geotechnical engineers to improve site-specific liquefaction hazard evaluations for gravelly deposits. The model and the curated database will serve as a benchmark for future research in complex soil behavior.
proposals.novelty: While ML has been applied to liquefaction prediction, few studies have focused specifically on gravelly soils or have systematically integrated detailed compositional data as primary model features alongside CPT readings. This approach moves beyond applying generic corrections to existing methods.

proposals.title: A Physics-Informed, Data-Driven Screening Framework for Liquefaction Hazard in Gravelly Soils Using DEM-CFD-Generated Training Data
proposals.motivation: Current liquefaction hazard screening for gravelly soils relies heavily on empirical correlations derived from sandy soil databases, which are often poorly constrained for coarse-grained materials. Field case histories, like those from the 2012 Emilia and 2020 earthquakes, show complex liquefaction behavior in gravels that is not fully captured by simplified CPT- or SPT-based methods. A more fundamental, mechanics-based approach is needed to improve preliminary hazard identification, especially for sites where conventional penetration tests are challenging.
proposals.hypothesis: The complex pore pressure generation and liquefaction resistance of gravelly soils under cyclic loading, as captured by high-fidelity DEM-CFD simulations, can be distilled into a simplified, rapid screening model using key state and gradation parameters. This model will outperform purely empirical methods by incorporating explicit particle-scale mechanics.
proposals.method: We will first conduct a parametric suite of 3D Discrete Element Method (DEM) coupled with Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD) simulations of cyclic direct simple shear on virtual specimens. Variables will include gradation (from sandy gravels to well-graded gravels), initial density (void ratio), and confining stress. The simulation outputs (stress-strain response, pore pressure buildup) will form a comprehensive training database. This database will then be used to train a machine learning model (e.g., a Gradient Boosting Regressor or a carefully structured Neural Network) to predict a normalized liquefaction resistance (CRR) based on a minimal set of inputs: measurable gradation shape parameters (e.g., coefficient of uniformity, fines content), state parameter (or relative density), and mean effective stress. The final tool will be a simplified calculation procedure or nomogram derived from the trained model.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, open-access screening framework that bridges the gap between high-resolution numerical simulation and geotechnical field practice. It will provide a more transparent and physics-based alternative to existing empirical methods for gravels, directly addressing the challenges highlighted in recent field case history studies. The framework will be validated against the documented case histories from the 2012 Emilia and 2020 earthquake liquefaction databases.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in using DEM-CFD not for direct site analysis, but as a controlled numerical laboratory to generate the fundamental data needed to train a rapid, interpretable screening model. This creates a physics-informed data-driven tool, a significant departure from both purely empirical methods and computationally prohibitive direct numerical simulation for routine practice.

proposals.title: Calibration of Synthetic Gravelly Soil Mixtures for Cyclic Response Prediction: A Fabric-Correction Framework from Direct Simple Shear Testing
proposals.motivation: Current liquefaction assessment for gravelly soils often relies on models calibrated with reconstituted, synthetic mixtures, which lack the complex fabric, particle shape, and stress history of natural deposits. This 'fabric gap' introduces significant uncertainty in predicting the cyclic strength and deformation of natural gravelly soils, as evidenced by case histories from events like the 2012 Emilia and 2020 earthquakes. Bridging this gap is critical for improving the reliability of seismic hazard analyses in regions with gravelly alluvial deposits.
proposals.hypothesis: The cyclic simple shear response of natural gravelly soils can be reliably predicted from tests on synthetic gravel-sand-fines mixtures by applying quantifiable correction factors that account for the systematic effects of in-situ fabric and geological history.
proposals.method: Undisturbed frozen samples and reconstituted specimens will be obtained from well-characterized field sites in the Po Plain, leveraging prior case history databases. These will be tested in parallel with manufactured mixtures of matching gradation in a cyclic simple shear device under identical stress-controlled protocols. The comparative analysis will quantify differences in cyclic resistance ratio (CRR), pore pressure generation, and strain accumulation. Statistical and machine learning techniques (e.g., multivariate regression or Random Forest models) will be used to derive fabric-correction factors as functions of measurable index properties and in-situ test data (e.g., CPTu).
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a directly applicable framework to calibrate numerical models and empirical correlations based on synthetic mixtures for use with natural gravelly soils. It will deliver a dataset linking in-situ characterization, fabric, and cyclic performance, and provide validated correction factors to reduce uncertainty in liquefaction triggering assessments for gravels.
proposals.novelty: The research moves beyond simple validation to a systematic calibration exercise, directly pairing natural and synthetic specimens under identical laboratory conditions to isolate and quantify the fabric effect, which is often acknowledged but rarely rigorously corrected for in engineering practice.

ideas.title: Characterizing the Transitional Cyclic Failure Envelope of Bioclastic Sands: From Stable Response to Flow Liquefaction
ideas.description: This research systematically maps the complete cyclic deformation and pore pressure response spectrum of deeper, bioclast-rich marine sands, moving beyond a binary liquefaction classification. It employs high-resolution cyclic laboratory tests coupled with in-situ fabric visualization via micro-CT to quantify how bioclast content, shape, and intraparticle porosity interact with confinement and drainage to govern the transition from cyclic mobility to flow liquefaction. The outcome will be a validated constitutive model that predicts strain accumulation and pore pressure rise for these materials under realistic seismic sequences, directly informing seismic hazard assessment for offshore infrastructure founded on calcareous deposits.

ideas.title: SDMT-constrained dynamic pore pressure modeling for performance-based ground improvement optimization
ideas.description: This research synthesizes dynamic pore pressure modeling with Seismic Dilatometer (SDMT) data integration to create a performance-based optimization framework for liquefaction mitigation. The core innovation is using pre- and post-event SDMT parameters (like material index, shear wave velocity, and horizontal stress index) to rigorously calibrate and validate advanced constitutive models that simulate pore pressure generation and dissipation in critical silt-rich layers during seismic events. The calibrated model then directly informs the optimization of ground improvement design parameters (e.g., column spacing, depth, and material stiffness) with the explicit objective of controlling the dynamic pore pressure response to meet predefined performance criteria, such as limiting residual excess pore pressure ratio. This moves beyond traditional, conservatively calibrated empirical methods to a site-specific, physics-informed design that is both more efficient and more reliably targeted at the actual seismic vulnerability mechanism.

ideas.title: Machine Learning-Augmented Forensic Stratigraphy for Liquefaction Source Attribution
ideas.description: This research fuses forensic soil analysis with multi-parameter machine learning to develop a novel framework for liquefaction source attribution. Machine learning models will fuse CPTu, DMT, and Vs data to create high-resolution 3D stratigraphic models and predict liquefaction susceptibility. When sand boils occur, forensic techniques (e.g., colorimetry, organic matter, mineralogy) will analyze their ejecta, and the ML model will inversely match this material signature to the most probable subsurface source layer within the predicted stratigraphy. This enables a closed-loop system that validates and refines liquefaction predictions by physically linking surface effects to their precise subsurface origins, improving both post-event forensic analysis and the spatial accuracy of future vulnerability assessments.

ideas.title: Spatial prediction of composition-dependent liquefaction susceptibility using geostatistical mineralogical kriging
ideas.description: This research fuses geostatistical kriging with the inverted soil composition hypothesis to develop a novel, spatially explicit model of liquefaction susceptibility. It treats critical mineralogical ratios (e.g., litharenite/quartz content) as regionalized variables, using sparse borehole and CPT data to interpolate their spatial continuity across a site. The resulting 3D model predicts where, within a continuous silty sand unit, the composition shifts towards more liquefaction-susceptible litharenitic endmembers. This enables probabilistic hazard maps that account for intrinsic, spatially correlated compositional heterogeneity, moving beyond uniform unit-based assessments.

ideas.title: Employ geostatistical kriging for layer correlation
ideas.description: Use geostatistical interpolation methods from mining geology to predict the spatial continuity of the liquefiable silty sand unit.

ideas.title: Joint Inversion of Surface Deformation and Geophysical Tomography for 4D Subsurface Liquefaction Characterization
ideas.description: This research fuses remote surface deformation monitoring with subsurface geophysical inversion into a single joint inversion framework. By integrating time-series InSAR or drone-based photogrammetry data with seismic or electrical resistivity tomography surveys, the method uses the measured surface heave/settlement as a direct constraint on the geophysical inversion process. This synergy allows for the spatially and temporally continuous estimation of critical subsurface engineering properties, such as void ratio redistribution and pore pressure dissipation, between and beyond sparse instrumented points. The resulting 4D model provides an unprecedented view of liquefaction triggering, effects, and remediation performance, significantly improving hazard assessment and treatment validation.

ideas.title: Employ a Multi-Modal Data Fusion Network for Liquefaction Prediction
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from medical diagnostics, where multi-modal data fusion (e.g., MRI, CT, genomics) is used for improved disease prediction. The analogous solution employs specialized neural network architectures with attention mechanisms to intelligently weigh and integrate heterogeneous data sources. Adapted to geotechnical engineering, the research will develop a multi-branch machine learning model. This model will have dedicated initial processing branches for Cone Penetration Test with pore pressure measurement (CPTu), Dilatometer Test (DMT), and Shear Wave Velocity (Vs) data. A central fusion layer, utilizing an attention mechanism, will learn to dynamically prioritize and combine the complementary information from these different in-situ tests based on the specific soil profile and loading conditions, leading to a significant improvement in liquefaction vulnerability prediction accuracy over single-method or simplistically fused approaches.

ideas.title: Fingerprinting Liquefaction Sources with Forensic DNA Mixture Deconvolution Techniques
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution to a parallel problem in forensic genetics—deconvolving DNA mixtures from multiple individuals—to the geotechnical challenge of fingerprinting liquefaction source layers. In forensics, high-throughput genetic markers and probabilistic genotyping models separate individual profiles from a mixed sample. Adapted to sedimentology, heavy mineral analysis and detrital zircon geochronology provide the diagnostic 'markers' for sediment sources. A new probabilistic modeling framework would be developed to treat the sediment in a liquefaction feature as a mixture. By inputting quantitative mineralogical and geochronological data from both the mixture and potential source layers, the model would statistically deconvolve the mixture, estimating the number of contributing layers and their proportional contributions, thereby providing a more precise and quantitative fingerprint of liquefaction sources than simple compositional matching.

ideas.title: Probabilistic Liquefaction Assessment for Heterogeneous Layered Systems via Coupled Stochastic-Numerical Modeling
ideas.description: This research develops a robust framework that moves beyond deterministic modeling of idealized layered systems. It first generates numerous geologically plausible realizations of the subsurface, incorporating spatially variable sandy cap thickness, permeability, and interface geometry using geostatistical methods. Each realization is then analyzed with the numerical model to simulate seismic response and liquefaction potential. By aggregating results across the ensemble, the approach outputs probabilistic hazard maps and fragility curves, directly quantifying uncertainty and identifying configurations most sensitive to heterogeneity. This provides a more reliable and risk-informed assessment tool for engineering practice.

ideas.title: Fabric- and History-Informed Calibration of In-Situ Tests Using Synthetically Engineered and Naturally Validated Soil Models
ideas.description: This research overcomes the critical flaw of neglecting natural soil fabric and history by developing a calibration framework that explicitly engineers these features. First, advanced lab techniques are used to create synthetic soil specimens with controlled fabric, cementation, and stress history. Second, the correlation between in-situ test parameters (from SDMT/CPTu) and fundamental soil properties is rigorously calibrated by comparing responses from these synthetic specimens against a benchmark database of results from high-quality natural soil samples. This dual approach ensures the lab-scale models capture the essential geomechanical features controlling in-situ behavior, leading to more reliable field interpretation.

ideas.title: Inverse Design of Ground Improvement Based on Site-Specific Deformation Risk Models
ideas.description: This research fuses the empirical and inverse approaches to create a next-generation design methodology for liquefaction mitigation. First, field test data is used to develop probabilistic models that correlate initial soil state (e.g., SPT, CPT, Vs) with expected post-liquefaction deformation magnitudes for untreated ground. These site-specific deformation risk models then define the target performance parameters (e.g., maximum allowable settlement or lateral spread) for the inverse problem. The inverse solver is subsequently tasked with calculating the necessary ground improvement specifications (e.g., column spacing, depth, material properties) required to modify the subsurface's initial state parameters sufficiently to achieve the deformation target, thereby directly linking treatment design to quantified performance outcomes.

ideas.title: DEM Investigation of Stratigraphic Heterogeneity on Sand Boil Source Composition and Initiation
ideas.description: This research combines the geological question of source layer continuity with grain-scale numerical modeling to fundamentally investigate sand boil genesis. Using the Discrete Element Method (DEM), a virtual stratigraphy with discontinuous silt and sand lenses will be constructed and subjected to seismic loading. The simulation will track the mobilization and mixing of grains from distinct layers during liquefaction, directly testing if boils are sourced from homogeneous or heterogeneous materials. This approach will reveal how stratigraphic interfaces and layer discontinuities act as nucleation points for boiling and control the particle-size distribution of ejecta.

ideas.title: Heterogeneity of Ground Improvement as a Substitute for a Non-Liquefiable Crust
ideas.description: This research challenges the conventional design paradigm by investigating if the heterogeneous composite created by ground improvement (e.g., stone columns, jet grouting) can itself act as a hydraulic and mechanical barrier, eliminating the assumed necessity for a pre-existing natural crust. It will evaluate whether the irregular, three-dimensional network of improved soil zones can effectively impede pore pressure dissipation pathways or redistribute strains to prevent damaging surface effects, even in their absence. The core novelty lies in treating the improvement's non-uniformity not as a design flaw but as a potential engineered system characteristic that substitutes for a specific geologic condition. This could lead to revised seismic design codes and more adaptable ground improvement strategies for sites lacking a natural crust.

ideas.title: Regional Port Network Resilience Modeling via Petrographically-Characterized Liquefaction Susceptibility
ideas.description: This research integrates the petrographic correlation method for mapping regional liquefaction susceptibility of silty Quaternary sequences with a port system risk assessment framework. It develops a geospatial model that links the subsurface vulnerability of coastal alluvial plains, identified through correlated sedimentology, to the functional nodes of a regional port network. This allows for the simulation of seismic scenarios where ground failure propagates disruption based on shared geological units, identifying critical bottlenecks and interdependencies. The outcome is a novel, geology-informed resilience plan for port systems, prioritizing mitigation where it most protects network-wide functionality.

ideas.title: Bridging the fabric gap: Calibrating synthetic mixture response against natural gravelly soils in cyclic simple shear
ideas.description: This research employs a comparative experimental framework to move beyond simple validation. Undisturbed and reconstituted samples of natural gravelly soils from characterized field sites are tested in parallel with manufactured gravel-sand-fines mixtures under identical cyclic simple shear protocols. The direct comparison quantifies the systematic differences in cyclic strength, pore pressure generation, and deformation mechanisms arising from fabric, particle shape, and geological history. These results are used to develop and apply correction factors or fabric-inclusion rules, thereby calibrating the numerical models tested on synthetic mixtures to reliably predict the behavior of natural deposits.

ideas.title: Physics-Constrained Causal ML for Generalizable Liquefaction Assessment
ideas.description: This research develops a hybrid modeling system that fuses machine learning with geotechnical first principles to predict liquefaction potential. The core innovation is a physics-informed neural network architecture trained not only on historical case data but also constrained by fundamental soil mechanics equations, ensuring predictions are physically plausible. A causal inference module identifies stable, non-spurious relationships between composition, in-situ state, and liquefaction response across diverse geological settings. Finally, an adaptive sampling strategy guides targeted field testing to iteratively expand the training database into regions of greatest model uncertainty, systematically improving robustness and generalizability beyond the original historical data distribution.

ideas.title: A DEM-CFD-Informed Rapid Screening Framework for Site-Specific Liquefaction Hazard
ideas.description: This research direction develops a novel two-tiered framework that synergistically couples high-resolution DEM-CFD simulations with a rapid screening tool. The core innovation is using extensive, pre-computed DEM-CFD simulations of cyclic loading on virtual soil specimens with varying gradations, densities, and stress states to train a simplified, data-driven screening model. This model distills the complex particle-scale mechanisms of pore pressure generation into a minimal set of key, measurable site parameters (e.g., gradation shape, state parameter) and a corresponding rapid test or calculation. The result is a physics-informed screening tool that provides faster, more mechanistically transparent preliminary hazard identification than purely empirical methods, while being vastly more practical for widespread use than direct DEM-CFD analysis.

ideas.title: Assessing the 'Observer Effect' in Ground Improvement: A Non-Destructive Testing Protocol
ideas.description: This research seed investigates the dual problem of whether the SDMT/CPTu testing process itself alters the properties of recently improved ground, a form of geotechnical 'observer effect'. Drawing an analogy from quantum physics, where measurement apparatus interaction with a system is a fundamental concern solved via non-perturbative techniques and comparative modeling, the adapted idea is to develop a coupled testing protocol. A non-intrusive geophysical method will first establish a baseline. Intrusive testing follows, after which the non-intrusive method is re-deployed. By comparing pre- and post-intrusion geophysical signatures and correlating them with the intrusive data, the research aims to quantify the extent and nature of ground property alteration caused by the testing tool itself, separating test-induced damage from the original ground state.

ideas.title: A Physics-Informed Hierarchical Framework for Pore Pressure Generation Incorporating Soil State and Fabric
ideas.description: This research develops a predictive framework for excess pore pressure ratio (ru) rooted in the principle of dissipated energy, moving beyond purely empirical correlation. The framework explicitly incorporates soil state and fabric parameters—such as a soil structure index, stress history, and initial anisotropy—as modular scaling functions that modify the energy-based core relationship. A comprehensive experimental program, utilizing advanced laboratory techniques (e.g., bender elements, tomography), generates a targeted database for calibrating and validating the model's components. The final output is a robust, mechanics-based model capable of reliable ru predictions for a wide range of natural and reconstituted soils under varied loading paths, significantly improving generalizability over site-specific correlations.

ideas.title: Dynamic Performance-Based In-Situ Assessment of Engineered Granular Fill with Minimal Disturbance
ideas.description: This research investigates a closed-loop framework where the dynamic properties of compacted gravel fills, designed for specific foundation performance (e.g., seismic isolation, machine foundations), are verified using minimally intrusive in-situ tests. The core innovation is to characterize the disturbance caused by tests like SDMT/CPTu not just as a problem, but as a calibrated input to a predictive model. By correlating the measured 'test-induced' changes in dynamic shear modulus and damping with the fill's initial gradation and density, the model can be inverted to recommend optimal adjustments to the fill design and compaction process, ensuring the as-built material meets the target dynamic performance criteria.

ideas.title: Geophysical Tomography for 3D Characterization of Gravelly Soil Improvement Across Diverse Geologic Settings
ideas.description: This research fuses geophysical tomography with the adaptation of ground improvement assessment for diverse gravelly soils. It aims to use cross-hole or surface-wave tomography to generate 3D volumes of shear wave velocity or other geophysical properties within improvement zones constructed in gravels of different geologic origins (e.g., alluvial, glacial, colluvial). These 3D tomographic images will be calibrated and validated against a spatially distributed network of Dynamic Cone Penetrometer (DCP) tests, extending the DCP-based framework. The core synergy is the creation of predictive, geology-specific models that link intrinsic soil fabric and depositional environment to the 3D spatial pattern of improvement achieved, moving beyond 1D point measurements to a comprehensive volumetric understanding.

ideas.title: City-wide liquefaction hazard reassessment via distributed sensor fusion and epidemiological risk mapping
ideas.description: This idea scales up liquefaction risk reassessment by drawing an analogy from public health epidemiology. The analogous problem is updating city-wide disease risk maps using distributed, real-time data aggregation and analysis. The adapted solution involves deploying a dense network of seismic and geotechnical sensors across an urban area, treating each sensor node as a source of 'surveillance data'. By fusing this continuous stream of in-situ measurements with existing geospatial models in a dynamic analysis framework, the research enables a near-real-time, continuously updated liquefaction hazard map. This approach directly measures and visualizes the effectiveness of past ground improvement interventions at a city scale, while simultaneously identifying emerging or persistent risk hotspots, transforming static hazard maps into a dynamic risk management tool.

ideas.title: Centrifuge Modeling of Cap Integrity Using Petrographic Source Fingerprinting
ideas.description: This research combines centrifuge physical modeling with detailed petrographic analysis to investigate the performance of low-permeability caps over gravel layers. The centrifuge tests will subject capped systems to high-g forces, simulating long-term loading and inducing potential cap failure. Crucially, the cap material will be engineered from distinct geologic units, and post-test forensic petrography (analyzing bioclast content and other signatures) will be used to uniquely identify the source of eroded or failed material. This approach tests whether cap failure is a generic mechanical issue or is uniquely tied to the specific mineralogical and textural properties of a source material, even under altered stress and saturation conditions.

ideas.title: Geological-process-classified, zone-adaptive geostatistics for liquefiable unit correlation
ideas.description: This approach first uses a supervised machine learning model, trained on borehole-derived sedimentological and geotechnical features, to classify the depositional environment at each control point and predict a spatial map of geological process zones. Within each identified zone (e.g., distributary channel, lacustrine delta), a local variogram is calculated and used to perform separate kriging interpolations. The final 3D model of the liquefiable unit is created by merging these zone-specific predictions, constrained by the boundaries of the interpreted depositional systems, resulting in a correlation that honors both spatial statistics and the non-stationary geological reality.

ideas.title: Apply forensic pollen profiling to sand boil sediment sourcing
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from forensic palynology, where pollen and spore assemblages are used as unique fingerprints to link evidence to specific locations. The analogous solution involves analyzing the specific mix of pollen grains to match a sample to a known geographic and seasonal profile. Adapted to geotechnical engineering and levee safety, the method would involve extracting and analyzing the pollen and spore content from sand boil sediment. Since different subsurface strata (e.g., a paleochannel fill vs. overbank deposits) were laid down in different eras with distinct vegetation, their pollen signatures are unique. By comparing the pollen 'fingerprint' of the ejecta to reference profiles from core samples of various layers, researchers can definitively trace the sand boil material back to the specific subsurface layer that liquefied, greatly improving the accuracy of seepage path identification and risk assessment.

ideas.title: Adapting Phylogenetic Biogeography to Map Liquefaction Susceptibility in Coastal Sequences
ideas.description: This idea adapts the problem-solving approach from phylogenetic biogeography in evolutionary biology to the geotechnical challenge of mapping regional liquefaction susceptibility. In biogeography, the distribution of species is explained by constructing phylogenetic trees (showing evolutionary relationships) and correlating lineage divergence with geological events. The analogous solution is adapted by classifying Quaternary silty units into distinct sedimentary facies 'lineages' based on petrographic and geotechnical fingerprints. Their spatial distribution is then mapped against the known chronology of marine-continental sequences (e.g., sea-level curves, paleo-shoreline positions). This creates a susceptibility map that is genetically informed, predicting areas where the depositional 'history' (e.g., rapid transgressive estuarine silt vs. progradational deltaic silt) leads to higher liquefaction potential, extending the petrographic method into a dynamic, history-aware framework for coastal alluvial plains.

ideas.title: Centrifuge Modeling of Capped Gravel Layers Using Astrophysical Accretion Disk Analogy
ideas.description: This research adapts the experimental methodology from astrophysical modeling of protoplanetary accretion disks to physically model the coupled response of a gravel layer with a low-permeability cap using centrifuge testing. In astrophysics, scaled rotating tank experiments are used to study the instabilities and transport in layered gas-and-dust disks by matching key dimensionless numbers under high centrifugal force. The adapted idea applies this principle: the gravel layer is analogous to the dense particulate mid-plane, and the cap is analogous to the overlying gas envelope. By designing a centrifuge model that correctly scales the stress and flow regimes, the experiment will instrumentally capture the coupled hydromechanical instabilities, interface shear, and fluid transport dynamics within the capped granular system, providing physical validation for numerical models of such geotechnical structures.

ideas.title: Applying Rheological Measurement Techniques to Track Dynamic Hydraulic Conductivity During Cyclic Loading
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption of constant hydraulic conductivity during seismic shaking by drawing an analogy from complex fluid rheology. In that field, the dynamic microstructural evolution of materials like colloidal suspensions under oscillatory shear is probed using techniques like small-amplitude oscillatory shear (SAOS), which measures viscoelastic moduli in situ. The adapted solution is to instrument a cyclic simple shear apparatus to function as a coupled rheometer-permeameter. During controlled cyclic loading, a small, steady hydraulic gradient is maintained across the soil specimen. By continuously monitoring the transient pore pressure response or flow rate with high temporal resolution, a dynamic hydraulic conductivity value can be calculated throughout the shaking event. This allows direct observation of how conductivity changes with shear strain amplitude, number of cycles, and frequency, and correlates these changes with simultaneously measured mechanical properties (shear modulus, damping) to identify the micro-mechanisms (particle rearrangement, fines migration, fabric evolution) responsible for the dynamic hydraulic response.

ideas.title: Mapping the Heterogeneous Composite: A Geophysical Imaging Approach to Ground Improvement
ideas.description: This research challenges the common geotechnical assumption that ground improvement creates a uniformly enhanced soil mass by proposing that the result is a heterogeneous composite. Drawing an analogy from medical imaging, where non-invasive techniques like MRI and CT scans map internal tissue heterogeneity to diagnose disease, this idea adapts that solution. It proposes using a suite of geophysical imaging methods (e.g., MASW, ERT) on a treated soil zone to create high-resolution, 2D or 3D maps of engineering properties such as stiffness and density. This approach will empirically characterize the actual spatial variability of improvement, moving beyond a simplistic uniform model to a quantified composite model that can be linked to treatment efficacy and design parameters.

ideas.title: Apply geostatistical kriging to liquefaction susceptibility
ideas.description: Use spatial interpolation methods from geostatistics to map liquefaction probability between boreholes.

ideas.title: Adapting Granular Flow Rheology from Food Processing to DEM-CFD for Cyclic Loading
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from food processing and powder technology, where DEM-CFD coupling is used to model the rheology of dense, cohesive granular suspensions under shear. The analogous solution involves modeling inter-particle cohesive forces within DEM and coupling them with a viscous fluid phase in CFD to predict macroscopic flow from microscopic interactions. Adapted to the geotechnical context of cyclic loading, the research will employ DEM-CFD coupling to explicitly model particle-scale interactions (including frictional contacts and weak bonds) with the pore fluid. By applying cyclic shear strains, the study will investigate how the evolving granular fabric disrupts local fluid flow, leading to the generation of heterogeneous pore pressure fields. This approach aims to provide a particle-scale mechanistic understanding of the initiation of phenomena like soil liquefaction during cyclic loading.

ideas.title: Robust, Risk-Based Drainage Design Under Uncertainty for Liquefaction Mitigation
ideas.description: This research develops a probabilistic design framework for drainage systems in gravelly fills that explicitly accounts for geotechnical and seismic uncertainties. The approach integrates stochastic modeling of soil permeability, seismic hazard analysis, and long-term performance degradation (e.g., clogging) to quantify the risk of pore pressure buildup failure. The design problem is reformulated as a reliability-based optimization that produces drainage solutions (e.g., layout, capacity) which are robust, meaning they maintain a target safety margin across the widest possible range of uncertain future conditions. This moves the field beyond deterministic factor-of-safety methods toward a quantifiable, risk-informed design standard.

ideas.title: Refine boundary curves for different risk levels
ideas.description: Develop probabilistic boundary curves for the DPT method to correspond to different levels of liquefaction triggering risk.
ideas.sourceTitle: Gravel Liquefaction Assessment Using the Dynamic Cone Penetration Test Based on Field Performance from the 1976 Friuli Earthquake

ideas.title: Probabilistic Design Optimization of Ground Improvement Using Spatially Conditioned SDMT Data
ideas.description: This research develops a robust framework for ground improvement design that explicitly addresses the uncertainty and spatial variability inherent in soil conditions. Site investigation data, primarily from Seismic Dilatometer Tests (SDMT), is used not for direct calibration but to condition random field models of key geotechnical parameters. These spatial models drive Monte Carlo simulations within a numerical optimization routine that seeks the most cost-effective improvement scheme. The final design is therefore optimized for both performance and reliability, ensuring robustness against localized weak zones and measurement uncertainty.

ideas.title: Use geostatistics to map DPT-based liquefaction risk
ideas.description: Apply kriging or other spatial interpolation techniques to create regional liquefaction hazard maps from discrete DPT soundings.
