ideas.title: Quantifying Fabric-Dependent State Variables in Gravel via 4D Micro-CT of Seepage-Induced Eruptions
ideas.description: This research applies time-resolved (4D) micro-computed tomography to experimentally capture the internal fabric evolution of coarse-grained, gravelly materials during seepage-induced failure processes like sand boil formation. By analyzing the full 3D particle kinematics, contact force chains, and pore network restructuring throughout the failure event, the study aims to identify measurable fabric tensors or connectivity metrics that act as necessary state variables controlling the dynamics. This direct, 3D observational evidence from a critical failure mode provides a physical basis to challenge the sufficiency of the effective stress principle for gravels, proposing a new constitutive framework where effective stress and fabric state jointly govern stability.

ideas.title: Employ Gaussian Process Regression from Machine Learning for Layer Correlation
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from machine learning and spatial statistics to the geotechnical problem of layer correlation. The analogous field is Machine Learning/Spatial Statistics, where Gaussian Process Regression (GPR) is used to model spatial data by defining a covariance kernel function that captures relationships based on distance, providing predictions with uncertainty estimates. The adapted idea applies the GPR framework to predict the spatial distribution of the liquefiable silty sand unit. By using a suitable kernel function informed by geological understanding, the method will interpolate between sparse borehole data to produce a continuous, probabilistic model of the unit's geometry and properties, directly quantifying prediction uncertainty for engineering decisions.

ideas.title: Causal Inference Framework for DMT Parameters and Ground Improvement Efficacy Using Bayesian Networks
ideas.description: This research develops a causal inference model, rather than a simple correlation, to assess the relationship between Dilatometer Test (DMT) parameters and post-treatment liquefaction resistance. A Bayesian network integrates pre- and post-treatment DMT data with detailed records of treatment processes (e.g., grouting parameters, execution quality) and inherent site conditions (e.g., stratigraphy, fines content) from carefully documented case histories. The framework explicitly accounts for confounding variables to isolate the causal effect of DMT-derived soil state indicators on the improvement outcome. This approach yields a robust, probabilistic prediction tool that quantifies uncertainty and provides actionable guidance for treatment design based on pre-treatment investigation.

ideas.title: Multi-Physics Constrained Tomography for Robust 3D Ground Improvement Verification
ideas.description: This redesigned approach overcomes the inherent non-uniqueness of geophysical tomography by fusing it with direct geotechnical measurements. A hybrid sensor network is deployed, combining surface or cross-hole geophysical arrays with a strategically placed grid of direct penetration tests or instrumented inclusions. A coupled inversion algorithm jointly processes both data types, using the direct measurements as high-fidelity constraints to reduce the solution space of the tomographic model. The result is a robust, quantitatively reliable 3D image of ground improvement properties (e.g., shear wave velocity, strength) that is consistent with both indirect geophysical signatures and direct physical evidence.

ideas.title: Probabilistic hydraulic tomography: Bayesian joint inversion of multi-source pore pressure excitations to map aquifer heterogeneity and boundary conditions
ideas.description: This approach moves beyond a single earthquake event to actively and passively excite the aquifer system using multiple known sources, including controlled pumping tests, natural tidal loading, and ambient seismic vibrations. A spatially distributed network of pore pressure sensors records the system's response to these different excitations, providing the data density needed to resolve spatial heterogeneity. A Bayesian inversion framework is then used to jointly estimate the spatially variable hydraulic conductivity field, specific storage, and uncertain boundary conditions, producing not just a single 'best-fit' model but an ensemble of probable models that quantify estimation uncertainty. This results in a robust, probabilistically constrained 3D characterization of aquifer properties essential for reliable groundwater flow and contaminant transport predictions.

ideas.title: Apply Graph Neural Networks to Model Soil Particle Interaction Networks for Liquefaction Prediction
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from computational chemistry and drug discovery to the problem of predicting soil liquefaction potential. In the analogous field, Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) are successfully used to model molecules as graphs (atoms as nodes, bonds as edges) to predict complex chemical properties from structural data. The adapted research idea applies this methodology to geotechnical engineering by reconceptualizing a soil mass as an interaction network: soil particles or compositional clusters become nodes with features (e.g., mineralogy, size, shape), and their physical contacts or proximity form the edges. A GNN model would be trained on historical case history data, learning to map this graph representation—alongside in-situ conditions like confining pressure and saturation—to the target liquefaction behavior. This approach aims to capture the meso-scale interactions and force chains that govern liquefaction, moving beyond purely empirical or simplified index-based correlations to a more physics-informed machine learning prediction based on compositional and structural topology.

ideas.title: Imaging-Driven Pore-Scale Reconstruction of Cyclic Loading Effects on Saturated Granular Hydraulics
ideas.description: This research uses synchronized cyclic triaxial loading and intermittent high-resolution micro-CT imaging to capture the 3D evolution of a saturated granular specimen's fabric. After each imaging stage, the digitized pore geometry is used for computational fluid dynamics simulations to calculate the effective hydraulic conductivity, isolating the impact of particle rearrangement and fines migration. This approach provides a direct, causal link between microstructure changes observed during shaking and the resulting hydraulic property evolution, overcoming the impossibility of direct transient permeability measurement.

ideas.title: Uniform improvement across entire treated zone

ideas.sourceTitle: Monitoring Ground Improvement Using the Seismic Dilatometer in Christchurch, New Zealand

ideas.title: Capillary Network Design for Soil Pore Pressure Dissipation
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution to fluid pressure regulation in biological capillary networks (angiogenesis) to the geotechnical problem of designing drainage to prevent liquefaction. In vascular biology, networks are optimized for efficient perfusion based on parameters like pressure and demand. The adapted idea is to design hierarchically branched, biomimetic drainage systems for gravelly fills. By optimizing the architecture (branching, diameters, density) using geotechnical governing parameters (seismic energy, permeability, fluid properties), the system can dissipate pore pressure more effectively than conventional grids, mimicking a low-resistance vascular network to prevent damaging pressure buildup and liquefaction.

ideas.title: Model cap rock as a composite material using effective medium theory
ideas.description: This idea challenges the homogeneous layer assumption for cap rocks by drawing an analogy from materials science. In that field, the properties of heterogeneous composite materials are modeled using effective medium theory, which mathematically derives effective bulk properties from the constituents' properties and microstructure. Adapted to this context, the research would involve characterizing a cap rock as a composite material with distributed elements like shale, fractures, and sand. By applying effective medium theory, we can develop a methodology to calculate a spatially variable, effective permeability tensor for the cap layer. This provides a more physically realistic model for seal integrity assessment and fluid migration predictions than a simple uniform layer assumption.

ideas.title: Adaptive Monitoring of Ground Improvement using a Biomarker-Inspired Framework
ideas.description: This research adapts the clinical model of long-term, adaptive patient monitoring to assess ground improvement durability. Drawing an analogy from medicine, where treatment efficacy is tracked via scheduled check-ups using biomarkers and imaging rather than constant intensive testing, we propose an adaptive framework for geotechnical monitoring. The solution involves establishing a post-construction baseline using initial SDMT/CPTu tests. A risk model then dictates a monitoring schedule where the timing and type of subsequent high-resolution tests (the 'check-ups') are triggered either by predictive models of environmental or loading stresses likely to cause degradation, or by anomalies detected by a network of low-cost, continuous sensors (e.g., piezometers, inclinometers) acting as surrogate 'biomarkers'. This optimizes resource use, focuses investigation on critical times and locations, and provides a more intelligent, long-term assessment of ground improvement performance.

ideas.title: Probabilistic Performance Framework for Engineered Soil Composites
ideas.description: This research develops a predictive, probabilistic framework to model ground-improved zones as engineered soil composites with quantifiable spatial heterogeneity. It combines advanced site characterization with stochastic finite element modeling to generate statistical distributions of key engineering parameters (e.g., strength, stiffness) within the treated volume. The output is a performance-based design methodology that allows engineers to explicitly account for improvement heterogeneity, calculating reliability indices for foundation settlement or slope stability rather than assuming uniform properties.

ideas.title: Apply synchrotron micro-CT and digital volume correlation to track sand boil fabric evolution
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from biomechanics, where synchrotron micro-CT and Digital Volume Correlation (DVC) are used to track 3D microstructural evolution in bone under stress. The analogous problem of visualizing internal deformation in a complex, porous medium under load is solved by performing in-situ mechanical testing inside the scanner and applying DVC to sequential image volumes. Adapted to geotechnical research, a sand boil fabric sample would be subjected to controlled hydraulic forces within a micro-CT compatible cell. By capturing 3D image stacks at different stages, DVC analysis would map the 3D kinematic evolution of the sand matrix and pore space, providing an unprecedented dynamic view of the fabric changes responsible for sand boil initiation and growth.

ideas.title: Correlating SDMT parameters with post-treatment performance

ideas.title: Integrated subsurface-state constrained remote sensing for attribution of treatment-induced deformation
ideas.description: This research system combines InSAR or drone-based photogrammetry with a dense, co-located network of in-situ sensors monitoring key subsurface states (pore pressure, moisture, temperature). A coupled hydro-mechanical model assimilates both data streams in near-real-time to predict the background deformation driven by environmental factors. The treatment-induced deformation is then isolated as the residual between the total remotely measured displacement and the model-predicted environmental component, enabling accurate attribution and quantification of the treatment's geotechnical impact.

ideas.title: Remote Sensing-Informed, Performance-Calibrated Ground Improvement Optimization
ideas.description: This research synthesizes remote sensing for pre-event mapping with SDMT data-driven design optimization to create a novel, spatially-aware calibration framework. InSAR and hyperspectral data are used to create high-resolution, pre-construction maps of liquefiable deposit variability across a site. These maps then serve as the spatially-distributed input parameters for ground improvement design models, which are continuously calibrated and optimized using in-situ SDMT measurements taken before, during, and after improvement. The result is a closed-loop system that not only optimizes the design procedure but also dynamically adjusts the type, intensity, and layout of ground improvement measures in response to the spatially heterogeneous conditions revealed by remote sensing, achieving a level of site-specific precision and efficiency unattainable by either approach alone.

ideas.title: Multi-scale characterization and upscaling framework for heterogeneous cap rock hydraulic properties
ideas.description: This research develops a systematic workflow to move beyond the homogeneous layer assumption by quantitatively characterizing cap rock heterogeneity. It integrates field-scale geophysical methods with borehole and core data to statistically model fracture networks and matrix property variations. The resulting discrete fracture network (DFN) models are then hydraulically upscaled to provide equivalent anisotropic permeability tensors and capillary pressure functions. This enables the direct integration of quantified heterogeneity into practical reservoir-scale simulations for robust containment risk assessment.

ideas.title: Coupled Numerical-Physical Modeling of Capped Gravel Layers: Calibrating Cap Behavior via Centrifuge
ideas.description: This research employs a hybrid experimental-numerical framework to overcome the fundamental scaling conflicts in modeling capped gravel layers. A centrifuge test is conducted on a carefully instrumented model to generate high-stress conditions on a prototype-scale cap material, providing direct data on its deformation and failure mechanisms. This physical data is used to rigorously calibrate a parallel, high-fidelity hydro-mechanical finite element model. The validated numerical model is then run at full prototype scale and time to predict the complete, coupled long-term system response, ensuring robust and scalable conclusions.

ideas.title: Apply Vascular Network Modeling from Physiology to Pore Pressure Diffusion
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from physiology and biomedical engineering, where vascular network modeling simulates blood flow and pressure propagation through elastic, hierarchical vessel networks. The analogous solution uses computational fluid dynamics in deformable conduits of varying scale to model pressure wave transmission. Adapted to geotechnical research, this approach applies pore network modeling to simulate fluid flow and pore pressure diffusion in realistic gravel-sand-fine mixtures by conceptualizing the pore space as a deformable, hierarchical vascular system. The method will incorporate principles of network impedance and transient wave propagation from hemodynamics to better model how pressure disturbances diffuse through the heterogeneous, multi-scale porous structure, improving predictions for applications like landslide triggering or foundation settlement.

ideas.title: Multivariate Quantitative Petrography and Microchemistry for Robust Stratigraphic Discrimination
ideas.description: This redesigned approach moves beyond a single, subjective petrographic observation to create a defensible diagnostic model for Unit 2. It combines high-resolution quantitative petrography (automated mineralogy, point-counting) with microchemical cement analysis to establish a unique, multi-parameter signature. Statistical validation is then performed on blind samples from other units, including those subjected to simulated alternative diagenetic conditions, to test the model's discriminatory power and ensure it is not fooled by convergent processes.

ideas.title: Challenging petrographic uniqueness through forensic fiber analysis methods
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that low bioclast signatures uniquely identify Unit 2 by adapting methods from forensic fiber analysis. In forensics, apparently unique fiber evidence is often matched to multiple sources when analyzed under varied conditions (different lighting, treatments, magnifications). Adapting this approach, the research would systematically vary petrographic observational conditions—including thin-section preparation techniques, microscope settings, and advanced imaging methods—across all geological units. This methodological variation may reveal that the 'low bioclast' signature thought to be unique to Unit 2 actually appears in other units under different analytical conditions, thereby challenging the assumption of a single source identification based on standard petrographic analysis alone.

ideas.title: 4D Performance Monitoring of Ground Improvement via Hybrid Sensor Fusion and Guided In-Situ Testing
ideas.description: This research establishes a multi-scale monitoring framework to overcome the spatial blindness of traditional point-testing. It deploys a permanent network of distributed fiber optic sensors (DFOS) within the improved ground mass to provide continuous, meter-resolution measurements of strain and temperature along entire treatment zones. This spatially continuous data stream is fused with periodic 3D geophysical imaging surveys and strategically triggered, high-value SDMT/CPTu soundings, which are directed to locations flagged by the DFOS or geophysical data as showing anomalous behavior. The result is a robust, adaptive system that captures both global trends and localised degradation mechanisms, fundamentally improving the reliability of long-term durability assessments.

ideas.title: Physics-Informed Machine Learning Fusion of Distributed Fiber Optic and Point Sensor Data for Unambiguous Geotechnical Failure Detection
ideas.description: This redesigned system overcomes the interpretative ambiguity of raw fiber optic sensing by embedding it within an intelligent analysis framework. Continuous strain and temperature data from distributed fiber optic cables is fused in real-time with readings from a sparse network of traditional point sensors (e.g., piezometers, tiltmeters). A physics-informed neural network, trained on synthetic data generated from site-specific geomechanical models, analyzes the combined spatio-temporal data streams to identify patterns predictive of specific failure modes. This approach transforms the fiber from a simple data logger into a diagnostic tool capable of distinguishing critical geotechnical threats from background noise, providing actionable early warnings with high confidence.

ideas.title: Apply Neurovascular Bundle Monitoring to Distributed Fiber Optic Sensing for Ground Stability
ideas.description: This idea adapts the biomedical solution for monitoring neurovascular bundles to geotechnical engineering. In medicine, sensor arrays along neural and vascular pathways provide continuous, distributed data on pressure and flow to preempt catastrophic events. By embedding distributed fiber optic sensing cables as an artificial 'sensor bundle' within improved ground, the system can monitor for the initiation of micro-fractures (analogous to micro-hemorrhages) and changes in pore pressure/fluid flow (analogous to blood flow changes). Analyzing the spatial and temporal patterns of dynamic strain and temperature perturbations along the fiber allows for the early detection and localization of instability, transforming the cable from a passive monitor into an active early-warning system for ground failure.

ideas.title: Closed-loop, sensor-informed dynamic optimization of engineered granular fills
ideas.description: This research fuses distributed fiber optic sensing with granular material optimization to create an adaptive framework for engineered foundations. Fiber optic cables embedded during compaction provide a continuous, high-resolution map of strain and temperature, directly measuring the dynamic response of the granular matrix under load. This real-time sensor data is used to calibrate and validate discrete element method (DEM) or advanced constitutive models, which then iteratively refine target gradation curves and compaction energy to achieve desired stiffness, damping, and bearing capacity. The synergy enables a shift from empirical, recipe-based compaction to a performance-driven, closed-loop process where the material's in-situ behavior directly guides and confirms the optimization of its mechanical properties.

ideas.title: Calibrating Ground Improvement Models via SDMT Data Using a Pharmacokinetic Feedback Loop Analogy
ideas.description: This research idea proposes to solve the problem of calibrating ground improvement design models by drawing an analogy from clinical pharmacology. In that field, pharmacokinetic models are optimized using real patient biomarker data (e.g., drug concentration in blood) to refine dosing predictions. The analogous solution is adapted by treating field SDMT data—which provides direct measurements of soil parameter changes before, during, and after ground improvement—as the geotechnical 'biomarkers.' These measurements are fed into design optimization models (e.g., finite element models for densification or consolidation) in a feedback loop. The procedure involves using the SDMT data to calibrate critical model input parameters, validate predictions, and subsequently optimize the design specifications (e.g., column spacing, treatment energy, sequence) for future projects, thereby closing the gap between empirical observation and theoretical design.

ideas.title: Hierarchical Multi-Scale Framework for Particle-Fluid Interactions in Cyclic Loading
ideas.description: This research employs a hybrid, hierarchical modeling system to overcome the prohibitive cost of domain-wide DEM-CFD. A continuum-scale FEM model, using an advanced constitutive law, first simulates the global cyclic loading problem to identify localized zones of interest, such as shear bands or liquefaction fronts. Full DEM-CFD coupling is then applied only within these critical sub-domains, with boundary conditions dynamically extracted from the continuum solution. The particle-scale data from these high-fidelity patches is used to continuously inform and calibrate the fabric-dependent parameters of the continuum model, creating a closed-loop, computationally efficient framework that captures both representative macro-scale behavior and validated micro-scale mechanisms.

ideas.title: Inverse Design of Ground Improvement via Optical Inverse Scattering Methods
ideas.description: This idea inverts the ground improvement design process by drawing a direct analogy to inverse problems in optical engineering. In photonics, one often specifies a target electromagnetic scattering profile and solves for the material structure that produces it. Adapted here, the researcher first defines the target geotechnical parameters (e.g., a specific Vs or KD profile with depth). An iterative computational optimization framework is then employed. Starting from an initial guess of improvement specifications (e.g., stone column grid, jet grouting volume), a forward model simulates the soil's mechanical wave response. An algorithm (like an adjoint-based optimizer) calculates the gradient of error between the simulated and target profiles and updates the design parameters. This loop continues, effectively 'solving backwards' to output the required ground improvement geometry and material properties needed to achieve the pre-defined target performance.

ideas.title: Constrained Multi-Objective Optimization for Geotechnical Design Specification
ideas.description: This system reframes the inverse design problem as a search for the optimal ground improvement strategy that satisfies target performance parameters. It employs a genetic algorithm or Bayesian optimization coupled with a high-fidelity forward model to explore the design space. The optimization is constrained by practical limits (e.g., maximum injectable volume, available column diameters) and minimizes competing objectives like material cost, energy consumption, and predicted uncertainty in the outcome. The final output is a Pareto front of feasible design specifications, providing engineers with a set of optimal trade-off solutions rather than a single, potentially unstable, inverse result.

ideas.title: Adapt Optical Coherence Tomography (OCT) signal processing for InSAR phase unwrapping in deformation monitoring
ideas.description: This idea applies analogical reasoning from biomedical imaging to remote sensing. In ophthalmology, Optical Coherence Tomography (OCT) faces the analogous problem of reconstructing accurate depth profiles from noisy, wrapped interferometric phase signals. The field has developed sophisticated multi-frame and path-following phase unwrapping algorithms that use spatial continuity and probabilistic models to resolve ambiguities. We propose to adapt these core algorithmic strategies from OCT to process Interferometric Synthetic Aperture Radar (InSAR) data for engineering-scale deformation monitoring. By translating the noise-robust phase processing techniques, we aim to improve deformation tracking over challenging terrains, providing more reliable before-and-after treatment assessments of surface heave and settlement using drone-based or satellite InSAR.

ideas.title: Causal Disentanglement of Proximity Effects in Computerized and Paper-Based Cognitive Assessments
ideas.description: This study uses a controlled experimental design to isolate the causal mechanisms behind potential interactions between the CPTu and SDMT. Participants are randomly assigned to different sequences and temporal spacings of the tests across multiple sessions, with some sessions including high-load cognitive filler tasks. The design explicitly tests for order effects, fatigue, and practice, while psychometric models analyze performance at both the task and latent construct level. This approach moves beyond correlation to determine if observed influences are due to measurement artifact or genuine cognitive interference, providing actionable guidance for optimal test administration.

ideas.title: Sequential Testing Interference: A Counterbalanced, Cross-Over Design to Isolate Order Effects in Neuropsychological Assessment
ideas.description: This research seed challenges the assumption that CPTu (Continuous Performance Test, identical pairs) and SDMT (Symbol Digit Modalities Test) measurements are independent when performed in close proximity. Drawing an analogy from pharmacokinetics, where drug interaction studies use cross-over designs to isolate sequence and carryover effects, this expanded idea proposes a methodological adaptation. The study will employ a counterbalanced, cross-over experimental design. Participants will be randomly assigned to different sequences of test administration (e.g., CPTu-then-SDMT vs. SDMT-then-CPTu), with controlled inter-test intervals that may include cognitive 'washout' periods. By rigorously analyzing performance differences attributable to test order, the research aims to quantify any interference or priming effects, thereby testing the fundamental assumption of measurement independence in sequential neuropsychological assessment batteries.
